en\u001f07.01.2022, Charly Giesen: Landu: 7 January 2022, Charly Giesen: Landing heading 55 degrees! (20-metre gradient from NE to SW)
7 January 2022, Charly Giesen: Landing only in direction 16! The path between the fields is passable (former Pirmasens gliding airfield)
7 January 2022, Charly Giesen: Landings only on Runway 34!
en\u001f07.01.2022, Charly Giesen: Sehr: 7 January 2022, Charly Giesen: Very short fields with varied vegetation and, in some places, ditches running between them!
en\u001f07.01.2022, Charly Giesen: Weite: 7 January 2022, Charly Giesen: Another landing site 800 metres to the east (the road can be crossed – watch out for people and vehicles!)
en\u001f07.03.2026, Markus Seelig: Bin a: 7 March 2026, Markus Seelig: I ended up there on 7 August 25. Everything’s great!
en\u001f07.05.2021, Alexander Keupp: Auf: 7 May 2021, Alexander Keupp: There is now a supermarket on the site of the Sillian Ost field, which was part of the SIP. The Östliche Wiese has fence posts and a power line at its eastern end. It can be used heading east if you touch down in the second half and roll out before the road (approx. 250m long). Only use this field if Sillian is not available for landing.
en\u001f07.05.2021, Alexander Keupp: Sch: 7 May 2021, Alexander Keupp: A narrow strip between a row of trees and the houses. When approaching from the west, there are tall trees on the left-hand side. On the right, near the last houses (towards the west), there are posts. Flat meadow ground, possibly wet (depression). Posts near the last house towards the west.
en\u001f07.05.2022, Sabine Wisbacher: Se: 7 May 2022, Sabine Wisbacher: A very friendly club! Landing with larger aircraft may be challenging due to space constraints. (Approach over tall trees; the actual landing distance is more like 350 metres)
en\u001f07.06.2022, Patrick Misun: Das F: 7 June 2022, Patrick Misun: Approach the field from the south-east, north-west of the road (slightly ascending).
en\u001f07.06.2022, Patrick Misun: Lande: 7 June 2022, Patrick Misun: The landing area extends along the north-western side as far as the road. Natural meadow.
en\u001f07.06.2022, Patrick Misun: Vorsi: 7 June 2022, Patrick Misun: Caution advised due to farming activities. Current height (7 June 2022): 10 cm. The line shown on the map does not exist.
en\u001f07.07.2025, Ute Baranowski: Besi: 7 July 2025, Ute Baranowski: Viewing on 8 July 2025. Condition as described
en\u001f070 Aerodrome prive. 20m de larg: 070 Private airfield. 20 metres wide. Grass. Slight incline towards the north-east.
en\u001f070/250 Prairies. Partie S souve: 070/250 Meadows. The southern section is often waterlogged. Walkers, please take care.
en\u001f08.03.2019, Christian Hynek: Die: 8 March 2019, Christian Hynek: The field by the factory can no longer be used!
en\u001f08.03.2021, Christoph Dolainsky:: 8 March 2021, Christoph Dolainsky: Management at NW
en\u001f08.03.2021, Helmut Zeferer: Land: 8 March 2021, Helmut Zeferer: The landing area is as described! OK 2021
en\u001f08.03.2021, Helmut Zeferer: Wie: 8 March 2021, Helmut Zeferer: As described in the SIP OK 2021
8 March 2021, Helmut Zeferer: As described in the SIP!
8 March 2021, Helmut Zeferer: As described in the SIP, ok 2021
en\u001f08.03.2021, Helmut Zeferer: ist: 8 March 2021, Helmut Zeferer: Can be landed as originally described in SIP, but with the light guide in the third third and the post in the second third! Left-hand course
en\u001f08.03.2021, Paul Bernhard: Guter: 8 March 2021, Paul Bernhard: In good condition, new aerial photograph.
en\u001f08.03.2021, Paul Bernhard: guter: 8 March 2021, Paul Bernhard: in good condition, recent aerial photograph.
en\u001f08.07.2026, Zoltan Kovats: Außen: 8 July 2026, Zoltan Kovats: Field landing on 22 March 2026. The southernmost field, just south of the power line, was approached from the east over the houses, allowing for a crosswind approach with a short final. Short wooden posts mark out the plots and are only visible during the final approach; they are barely visible at all, particularly when the grass is tall.
en\u001f08.07.2026, Zoltan Kovats: Gelan: 8 July 2026, Zoltan Kovats: Landed on 7 August 2025; several large and small power lines are not a problem as long as you can see them; low wooden posts mark out plots and are only visible late in the final approach, but leave sufficient space between them
en\u001f080/260 Parcelles paralleles. Ch: 080/260 Parallel plots. A farm track divides the agricultural zone into two sections, creating a 50 cm difference in height. Tailwind on the southern side of the field.
en\u001f085 Piste privee. Herbe. Atterro: 085 Private runway. Grass. Landing facing east. Right-hand turn. 10% gradient.
en\u001f09.01.2022, Reinhold Ortner: der: 9 January 2022, Reinhold Ortner: Crane currently on site: if approaching from the west, you must fly round it.
en\u001f09.03.2021, Gerhard Schweiger: D: 9 March 2021, Gerhard Schweiger: The field remains unchanged; there are no additional obstacles. However, in the event of a strong southerly wind, this alternative landing site is not recommended! Very strong downdrafts! The Sankt Aegyd outlanding field (aircraft carrier) is preferable.
9 March 2021, Gerhard Schweiger: The meadow remains unchanged. There are no obstacles, but grazing livestock is possible (cows).
en\u001f09.03.2024, Andreas Weitz: Landu: 9 March 2024, Andreas Weitz: Landing in March 2024 on a meadow north-east of the specified meadow. The meadow is approximately 350 metres long and 100 metres wide, and very flat. At the north-eastern end of the meadow there is a fence which must be flown over in a heading of 22. The farmer wants money (the meadow is free of ripples in March, but has some minor unevenness). [ WeGlide flight ]
en\u001f09.04.2013, Fichtl Norbert: Bin: 9 April 2013, Norbert Fichtl: I landed in Nassereith Nord on 27 July 2012. The power line 20 metres before the crossroads is now marked with an orange ball in the centre of the approach path. The meadow belongs to the retirement home right next door and is leased out. If the grass is long (as it was when I landed), the tenant wants compensation.
en\u001f09.04.2023, Herwig Wagner: Gute: 9 April 2023, Herwig Wagner: A good option; the two fields to the west are also suitable for landing
en\u001f09.07.2023, David Achtzehnter: W: 9 July 2023, David Achtzehnter: Due to the slope and the tall trees at the far end, only an approach from the south-east is recommended. During model aircraft flying sessions, the marked meadow may be unsuitable, as it is on the visitors’/car park side of the fence. On the other side of the fence (the model aircraft runway and the meadow next to it), however, it is also possible to land safely (parallel to the marked meadow), although on the right-hand side of the meadow there is a significant slope to the right (the area marked in red could be extended slightly towards the north-west) --> with the main wheel near the path, there is no problem. The photos are of this alternative.
en\u001f09.07.2024, Benedikt Bursian: Ge: 9 July 2024, Benedikt Bursian: Landed on 9 July 2024 north of the power line marked north of Field 1. The meadow rises slightly towards the west; there is a fence post in the centre of the meadow. The eastern part of Field 1 is divided into smaller fields, which are marked with (possibly variable) fence posts. Take care to avoid the power lines already marked on the map when approaching.
en\u001f09.12.2021, Marcus Neubronner: I: 9 December 2021, Marcus Neubronner: I have another aerial photo from 21 June 2021, showing the aeroplane on the meadow and the power line marked on the image. The meadow is a good place to land and presents no problems.
en\u001f09.12.2021, Marcus Neubronner: S: 9 December 2021, Marcus Neubronner: A very good field, divided into a total of four sections. Field 1 (approach 34) or Field 2 (approach 16) are preferred. Fields 3 and 4 tend to be a bit marshy and uneven, but are still usable. The paths bordering the fields are popular with walkers.
090/270 Landing in both directions. Watch out for stakes. West-facing: do not land just past the path: poor terrain.
en\u001f090/270 Terrain prive. Piste en: 090/270 Private land. Marked grass pitch. https://www.vaeivola.it
en\u001f090/270 Terrain prive. Vent Arri: 090/270 Private land. Tailwind from the south. On the final approach to 27, there may be turbulence; touch down at the air traffic control tower.
en\u001f10.03.2019, Werner Weninger: Bei: 10 March 2019, Werner Weninger: When approaching, watch out for a row of trees; the meadow slopes slightly to the right.
en\u001f10.03.2019, Werner Weninger: Mei: 10 March 2019, Werner Weninger: My first choice is definitely a north-easterly landing, because the meadow slopes slightly upwards. You shouldn’t drive over the paths running perpendicular to the landing direction. However, they are spaced far enough apart that you only need to choose one section. There are markers scattered across the meadow, but they are clearly visible.
en\u001f10.03.2019, Werner Weninger: Wen: 10 March 2019, Werner Weninger: If you’re coming from the north, don’t touch down too early because of a wave.
en\u001f10.04.2022, Andreas Wolfsgruber:: 10 April 2022, Andreas Wolfsgruber: Blocked, can no longer land!
en\u001f10.07.2009, Dietmar Weingant: Wi: 10 July 2009, Dietmar Weingant: As I learnt today (8 July 2009), a gas pipeline is being laid (it hasn’t been dug up yet, but the route has been marked out) and the meadow won’t be usable to the same standard for a few weeks.
en\u001f10.09.2023, Manuel Valenta: Foto: 10 September 2023, Manuel Valenta: Photo from 10 September
en\u001f10.11.2003, Hans Fitterer: Siche: 10 November 2003, Hans Fitterer: Certainly not a ‘normal’ off-airfield landing site, but perfectly suitable for a safe landing; however, it is relatively damp and cannot be accessed by car; this means the aircraft should be dismantled and carried to the trailer. It is essential to inspect the site on foot and from the aircraft. When approaching from the south at a low altitude, the field remains out of sight until the very end. – Landing from the south is possible, though visibility is poor due to the forest; landing from the north may also be possible.
en\u001f10.11.2003, Mathias Schunk: Dies: 10 November 2003, Mathias Schunk: This field should only be considered as an absolute emergency landing site. It is situated just 2.5 km from the Spitzingsattel, which is at the same altitude. With a reasonably sensible approach plan for this field, it is therefore also possible to fly safely over the Spitzingsattel, which means that Fischhausen (1.22.), just 4 km away and situated at 800 m, is available as a normal alternative landing site.
en\u001f10.11.2003, Mathias Schunk: Nur: 10 November 2003, Mathias Schunk: It should not be included in flight planning simply as an emergency landing site. The area between Geitau and Kufstein should be regarded as unsuitable for landing!
en\u001f10.12.2022, Michael Henökl: Agra: 10 December 2022, Michael Henökl: Agricultural land, plain and simple
en\u001f10.12.2022, Michael Henökl: Stro: 10 December 2022, Michael Henökl: Power line running close to and parallel to the strip. A 4-metre-high lamppost immediately adjacent to the landing area. Agricultural land
en\u001f100/280 Terrain prive. Piste en: 100/280 Private land. Marked grass pitch. www.aeroclubcorradogex.com
en\u001f105/285 Vent arriere cote S du c: 105/285 Tailwind on the south side of the field. An area frequented by walkers.
en\u001f11.04.2021, Martin Embacher: Der: 11 April 2021, Martin Embacher: My initial impression has been confirmed: the meadow is relatively flat along an axis extending about 30 metres, but slopes at an average gradient of about 10 per cent from the north-east (towards the valley) to the south-west. There are two options: fly from the north-east over the power line (10 metres high) and land at the farm at the latest, or land beneath the power line. In the lower section, however, there are more uneven patches and the wooden pole does pose quite a tricky obstacle. Anyone needing to use the meadow will hopefully already be 100 per cent focused anyway – and that’s exactly what’s required.
en\u001f11.05.2026, Simon Lemmerer: Situ: 11 May 2026, Simon Lemmerer: The situation remains the same; the approach is extremely difficult due to the V-shaped valley. Landing here is a real challenge; it’s very difficult to turn round in the valley towards Hieflau as there’s almost no space. If you manage that, I think it’s quite OK to land there. The meadow is very undulating, but the last third is OK and slopes gently uphill. You must land from west to east.
11 May 2026, Simon Lemmerer: The situation remains the same; a visit on a day with a light southerly foehn, with a strong headwind on landing.
en\u001f11.07.2021, Albert Kiessling: Di: 11 July 2021, Albert Kiessling: Gerhard Hiemesch’s description is incorrect; it should read: ‘Runway 27 is better’. However, the arrow in the picture is correct.
en\u001f11.07.2021, Benedikt Bursian: Gr: 11 July 2021, Benedikt Bursian: Most of the meadow is now a maize field; the remaining part of the meadow is very steep and can no longer be used for landing.
en\u001f11.09.2023, Dr. Arno Hutter: Bes: 11 September 2023, Dr Arno Hutter: Site visit on 10 September 2023. Small patches of vegetation to the west of the farmhouse, as can also be seen on the satellite image. Otherwise, currently only meadows.
11 September 2023, Dr Arno Hutter: Site visit on 10 September 2023. In the north-eastern section, parallel to the path, there is now a field
en\u001f11.10.2003, Christian Hynek: Da: 11 October 2003, Christian Hynek: As the meadow lies in a rather narrow valley, the view is a bit odd and manoeuvring is restricted. It has proved best to take up position north of the meadow and then fly eastwards via the west. The meadow is separated by a dirt track with a small transformer house, hence the name ‘Trafowiese’. It is best to land just past the track; this gives you enough space to glide out. However, aircraft have also landed in front of the track without any problems.
en\u001f11.10.2003, Christian Hynek: Um: 11 October 2003, Christian Hynek: To drive right up to the meadow, you need a key. You can probably get one from one of the houses on the access road.
en\u001f11.10.2003, Florian Forcher: Sch: 11 October 2003, Florian Forcher: It’s a shame – yesterday I was sad to discover that the lovely meadow had been ploughed up and is now being used to grow winter barley. The field to the left of this one has also recently been ploughed up. In the photo, you can see a slight dip on the left (= west); there are also two landing options there, each on a strip of meadow approximately 20–30 metres wide. To the west of the hedges in the centre of the picture, I also found a suitable meadow yesterday with a north-westerly landing direction. So: the meadow is gone, but there are still several landing options left, so do check in good time to see if anything else has been ploughed up.
en\u001f12.03.2004, Thomas Keuschnig: Ac: 12 March 2004, Thomas Keuschnig: Please note: concrete plinth in the northern meadow (red circle).
en\u001f12.03.2004, Thomas Keuschnig: me: 12 March 2004, Thomas Keuschnig: of the several landing meadows south-west of Hermagor, the one east of Hermagor is preferred as it is considerably larger and, thanks to the varied vegetation, there is always a suitable meadow available; obstacle-free approach (just watch out for the high-voltage power line)
en\u001f12.03.2004, Thomas Keuschnig: se: 12 March 2004, Thomas Keuschnig: a very large area, but please note that this is the only meadow without a power line or fence posts. There is generally a strong easterly wind, so land away from the valley. The meadow is bordered by a fence at both ends. It is easily recognisable from the air by the distinctive parallel features: the ditch on one side and the power line on the other.
en\u001f12.03.2021, Christoph Dolainsky:: 12 March 2021, Christoph Dolainsky: Sorry – that can’t quite be right. The new frequency is: 135.010. According to the list published by DAEC and on the ICAO chart.
en\u001f12.03.2023, Manuel Valenta: FSW: 12 March 2023, Manuel Valenta: FSW Landewiesen Tour: everything’s fine, as described
12 March 2023, Manuel Valenta: FSW Landewiesentour: everything’s fine, as described. Watch out for the change in terrain in the west!
12 March 2023, Manuel Valenta: FSW Landewiesen Tour: Everything was fine, just as described
12 March 2023, Manuel Valenta: FSW Landewiesentour: Everything was fine, as described. Definitely CAT C
12 March 2023, Manuel Valenta: FSW Landewiesentour: Everything was fine, just as described. Neuberg is the better option
12 March 2023, Manuel Valenta: FSW Landewiesen Tour: Everything was fine, as described. Very challenging; alternatives are to be preferred
12 March 2023, Manuel Valenta: FSW Landewiesentour: Everything was fine, as described. In our opinion, the western field was slightly better.
12 March 2023, Manuel Valenta: FSW Landewiesentour: Everything’s fine. Very good towards 11. Towards 29, the approach through the terrain is more challenging (Cat. B towards 29)
12 March 2023, Manuel Valenta: FSW Landewiesentour: The field slopes northwards and is undulating; in our opinion, the other meadows in Pernitz are preferable
en\u001f12.05.2023, Erich Schleifer: Bes: 12 May 2023, Erich Schleifer: Visited on 12 May 2023. The meadow is suitable for landing: long, wide and flat, rising very slightly towards the north. The valley is too narrow for a normal traffic pattern. A video is available here:
12 May 2023, Erich Schleifer: Visited on 12 May 2023. The meadow is still accessible; no changes to the original description. Click here for the video:

12 May 2023, Erich Schleifer: Inspected on 12 May 2023. The meadow is still suitable for landing, but challenging. 180 m past the threshold (= lakeshore), the terrain rises steeply. Approaching the site, several gaps between individual trees are visible, but only one will save your glider. Running parallel to the landing direction, there are ditches on the left and right, approximately 150 m apart. Video:
en\u001f12.06.2023, Marcus Neubronner: A: 12 June 2023, Marcus Neubronner: A good field in itself. The approach into the valley is somewhat challenging due to the high-voltage power line, although there is no need to fly over or under it. It runs almost parallel to the field. On approach, however, there is a small hill when approaching from the north; from the south, you fly over the houses. There is a windsock on the western side of the field. At the south-eastern end of the meadow, there is a metal post protruding from the grass. Caution!

12 June 2023, Marcus Neubronner: Approaching from the north to the south seems to be easier. When approaching from the south, you have to fly over a tall row of trees at the edge of the meadow. Otherwise, it’s a good landing site.
en\u001f12.08.2025, Marcus Lankenau: Hab: 12 August 2025, Marcus Lankenau: I chose the meadow south-east of the power line. The meadow becomes very undulating towards the west, towards the power line. [ WeGlide flight ]
en\u001f12.12.2021, Alexander Keupp: Das: 12 December 2021, Alexander Keupp: The main field (Germalmoos crossroads) is still easy to land in (meadow). The alternative listed in the catalogue was planted with maize at the time of the site visit. Fields south of the road offer a good alternative.
en\u001f12.12.2021, Alexander Keupp: Die: 12 December 2021, Alexander Keupp: The field behind the Edeka (red arrow) is a good alternative. Just a few gentle undulations. Land just past the crossroads (ditch – see picture, towards the Edeka). There’s a post at the start of the meadow on the left-hand side (see photo).
en\u001f12.12.2021, Alexander Keupp: Zwi: 12 December 2021, Alexander Keupp: Trees have been planted between the two landing areas (see photo). The meadow to the west of the road is a good alternative when the wind is from the north (landing towards the quarry).
en\u001f120/300 Terrain ULM. Piste balis: 120/300 Microlight airfield. Marked runway. Threshold offset by 30 (280 m).
en\u001f13.02.2021, ASKÖ Flugsport-Wien:: 13 February 2021, ASKÖ Flugsport-Wien: Landewiesentour 2020 – everything went well!
en\u001f13.03.2022, Reinhold Ortner: Wie: 13 March 2022, Reinhold Ortner: Found as described
en\u001f13.04.2021, Marcus Neubronner: F: 13 April 2021, Marcus Neubronner: The field can no longer be used, as half of it has been planted with fruit trees!
en\u001f13.05.2021, David Richter-trumme: 13 May 2021, David Richter-Trummer: I took another look at the meadow the other day whilst driving past. There are still no buildings there; it looks huge and seems very suitable.
en\u001f13.05.2022, Marcus Neubronner: A: 13 May 2022, Marcus Neubronner: Please note: The landing direction must always be 22, due to the rising terrain. The information previously entered stating a landing direction of 04 is incorrect!! A challenging field. Fly a crosswind approach along the slope north of the meadow. In the final approach, watch out for the power line at the start of the meadow! Fly over this and land uphill, parallel to the sports pitch. You can roll onto the path at the top end if the meadow isn’t long enough. But WARNING: there are often walkers on the path, and agricultural traffic comes and goes from time to time.
en\u001f13.05.2022, Marcus Neubronner: B: 13 May 2022, Marcus Neubronner: Ground photos added. Do not land on the path, but on one of the four fields (see aerial photo).
en\u001f13.05.2022, Marcus Neubronner: L: 13 May 2022, Marcus Neubronner: A long meadow, but the approach is through a very narrow valley (no crosswind approach; the meadow is not always fully visible when approaching from the west) and, when approaching from the west, over a tall row of trees on the eastern edge of the meadow.
en\u001f13.05.2022, Marcus Neubronner: W: 13 May 2022, Marcus Neubronner: Inspected the field on 7 May 2022. Still in very good condition and easy to land on. Land facing north if possible! Please announce your landing at least 5 minutes in advance on 122.555 MHz and report your final approach via blind report.
en\u001f13.05.2022, Vladislav Voron: Bin: 13 May 2022, Vladislav Voron: I landed here yesterday with ASK23. A very pleasant approach. You just need to watch out for the power lines to the south of the field. But these are very easy to spot.
en\u001f13.05.2023, Erich Schleifer: Bes: 13 May 2023, Erich Schleifer: Site visit on 12 May 2023. Meadow unchanged.
en\u001f13.06.2021, Manfred Schwegler: B: 13 June 2021, Manfred Schwegler: For the eastern (main) meadow, please REMOVE THE WEST ARROW from the OpenStreetMap map. As described in the text, only landing direction 10 (EAST) is possible on this meadow! When approaching from the east, the tall trees (wooded area) to the east of the road are an obstacle. However, a westward landing is possible on the adjacent meadows.
en\u001f13.07.2022, Benjamin Bachmaier:: 13 July 2022, Benjamin Bachmaier: The layout of the meadows seems to be slightly different today – the area best suited for landing is now a little further north-west. The area outlined in red is a large field which, when mown, is very easy to land on, far away from any high-voltage power lines.
en\u001f130/310 Choisir champ en fct cul: 130/310 Choose a field based on the crops grown.
en\u001f130/310 Terrain prive motoplaneu: 130/310 Private glider airfield. Power lines and aerials to the north-west. Marked runway.
en\u001f130/310 Terrain prive. Treuil. C: 130/310 Private land. Winch. Radio contact essential. Right-hand TDP. Touch down on 13 after the hump halfway down the runway. In a marked NW breeze, touch down short on 31
en\u001f14.02.2004, Ulrich Plarre: Wiese: 14 February 2004, Ulrich Plarre: The meadows north of the Witrschaftsweg are undulating and criss-crossed with ditches. This is livestock farming country!
en\u001f14.02.2025, Michael Huber: Anbei: 14 February 2025, Michael Huber: Please find attached a few recent photos of the meadow taken on 6 February 2025. The meadow is situated between a busy main road to the east and a farm track to the west. There is an embankment facing the main road. At the northern end of the meadow, facing the farm track, there is a road sign. To the south, it borders a field (with no ditch in between). About one-third of the way across, a small wooden post has been driven into the ground. It is marked in red and visible in the photo, but is obscured when the vegetation is taller.
en\u001f14.03.2009, Ulrich Plarre: Die W: 14 March 2009, Ulrich Plarre: The meadow slopes gently from west to east! It is situated south-east of the village of St-Ulrich am Pillersee, in the district of Straß. Watch out for high-voltage overhead lines and fences. There are no high-voltage lines on the approach!

14 March 2009, Ulrich Plarre: The meadows lie between Leogang and Lofer and the Steinernes Meer. Weißbach can also be approached from a heading of 120° (e.g. Föhn!), but in that case you must fly under or round the overhead power line.

14 March 2009, Ulrich Plarre: Taking the high-voltage power lines into account, the meadows are suitable and can be classified as ‘yellow’ in terms of their characteristics. The main landing area shown on the map should be approached from west to east, with a crosswind approach over a smaller high-voltage power line.To the north, towards the Inn, there is a change in terrain and the high-voltage power line lies at a lower elevation. At the start of the landing field, a low vegetation line must be taken into account. For the marked alternatives, the cross-country routes are suitable for taxiing.

14 March 2009, Ulrich Plarre: The meadows rise slightly from north to south! A normal approach to the landing area is not possible! The crosswind approach must be very close to the northern slope. The final approach will be very short! At the western landing fields, the crosswind path can be driven over. At the meadows by the Sessel lift, the crosswind path is a bank and cannot be driven over!
en\u001f14.03.2009, Ulrich Plarre: Diese: 14 March 2009, Ulrich Plarre: This field is situated to the west of a roundabout north of Bad-Hofgastein! Piles of manure are scattered irregularly! The field is smoothest near the stream! The undulation marked with a dotted line running diagonally across the entire field can be cycled over, with the exception of the spot in the middle!
en\u001f14.03.2009, Ulrich Plarre: Marka: 14 March 2009, Ulrich Plarre: A striking feature of the Landefeld is that the main access road disappears into a tunnel to the north-west of the Landefeld, whilst a lift on the western edge heads south up the mountain! There are large hotels on the western edge! It rises gently from east to west!
en\u001f14.03.2009, Ulrich Plarre: Varia: 14 March 2009, Ulrich Plarre: Option 1. Is this the better outfield landing site? The approach from the south crosses a slightly elevated stream bed with trees and a 380-kV power line that does not protrude. The approach from the north passes over the houses in Urreiting. To the west, there is a major high-voltage power line running parallel to the landing field. To the east, the field is bordered by a solid pasture fence that does not cause any interference!
en\u001f14.03.2009, Ulrich Plarre: Wiese: 14 March 2009, Ulrich Plarre: Meadow 2: Ski slope! Steep incline! Approach over a group of trees! Plenty of speed! It’s better to the west of the lift! The L-SPATZ has landed safely here once before in a real emergency! The marked power line is lower down and doesn’t interfere with the approach!

14 March 2009, Ulrich Plarre: Meadow 3: In the south-east of Steinberg, the slope is less steep, but you have to position yourself precisely! Approach via a triangle formed by three hay huts, heading straight for the top of the church tower! There is a steep embankment just before the threshold! The crosswind path can only be cleared by flying directly over the centreline.

14 March 2009, Ulrich Plarre: Meadow 1: Approach over the church tower and across a triangle of power lines! Touch down late, gaining speed as you go up the slope.! The upper part of the meadow becomes flatter! Before the marked landing area: rollable waves!
en\u001f14.03.2014, Werner Weninger: Bes: 14 March 2014, Werner Weninger: The description is accurate. The meadow slopes gently downwards.
en\u001f14.03.2014, Werner Weninger: Ich: 14 March 2014, Werner Weninger: It took me half an hour and two sets of directions to find this meadow, as it’s situated on a high plateau. Then, out of nowhere, a meadow 400 metres long and 100 metres wide opens up.
en\u001f14.03.2014, Werner Weninger: Lan: 14 March 2014, Werner Weninger: As in all mountain valleys, the narrow terrain makes it difficult to choose a landing spot. The meadow is also quite narrow.
en\u001f14.03.2014, Werner Weninger: Wie: 14 March 2014, Werner Weninger: As described, the field is very uneven. 600 metres further east, on the other side of the road, there is a field 300 metres long, which could be approached from either side.
en\u001f14.05.2023, Johannes Schwemmer:: 14 May 2023, Johannes Schwemmer: Visit by car on 12 May 2023 to the Unterwössen cross-country flying week. The Embach meadow is difficult to approach as you’re flying uphill past trees and a fence. If you have too little momentum, you’ll land roughly on the slope once you’ve cleared the fence. If you have too much momentum, you’ll fly over the crest, past the high-voltage pylons and the houses behind them. The Embach meadows are a stopgap solution anyway. Anyone who can’t make it to Zell am See – perhaps due to headwinds – should first aim to land on the lovely meadows in St. Johann im Pongau, 20 km away, as they’re flat. If that’s not enough either, you’re in a bit of a pickle. In our opinion, the lesser of two evils is then Embach East. After all, right next to this meadow (Embach) stands an impressive fire station – help shouldn’t be far away. Stefan and I weren’t entirely sure whether this was a C-class landing area or not a landing area at all. Hopefully, no one will have to decide that whilst flying a glider. Video here – please note, this isn’t Embach Ost but Embach; we initially got them mixed up when we were there.

14 May 2023, Johannes Schwemmer: Visited by car on 12 May 2023 during the Unterwössen cross-country flying week. Everything that’s already been written is correct. We only saw one pile of manure, which wasn’t a problem. But there were loads of annoying little marker posts. The bump in the ground mentioned was only faintly visible due to the tall grass, but it was a nuisance and a hazard. There are better meadows in the Gastein Valley, so in our opinion this one doesn’t deserve an ‘A’. See for yourselves:

14 May 2023, Johannes Schwemmer: Visit by car on 12 May 2023 to the Unterwössen cross-country flying week. As already mentioned in the comment on the Embach meadow: it’s better to land at St. Johann im Pongau. If you can’t make it there or to Zell, this meadow is probably the lesser of two evils compared to the Embach meadow. See the video – landing under the power line seems to us to be possible only for experts in calm conditions. It’s better to come to a halt in the ditch and against the fence at just moderate speed; you can easily survive that, as a rollover is unlikely. And if it works out, you’ll stand there feeling like a hero. Good luck! As I said: a hero lands in St. Johann. Video:

14 May 2023, Johannes Schwemmer: Visit by car on 12 May 2023 during the Unterwössen cross-country flying week. Meadow Plan A (blue) is easy to approach from the south-east. From our perspective, it’s a straightforward A-meadow. Video: (Even though Dorfgastein is mentioned here, it is actually Hofgastein.) Sat-nav destination: ‘Parkplatz Bertahof Bad Hofgastein’. Just south of the car park, which is on the west side of the main road, there is an entrance to the meadow on the east side of the road, which even seemed suitable for trailers. Alternative meadow (yellow): shorter but usable.

14 May 2023, Johannes Schwemmer: Visit by car on 12 May 2023 during the Unterwössen cross-country flying week. Meadow Plan A (blue) is easy to approach from the west (straight) or south (90-degree landing turn). Video showing the current condition here; it’s slightly uphill but appears to pose no problems. The alternative field (yellow) is shorter but usable. When approaching from the east, it’s best to fly parallel to the power line, which runs along the southern edge of the field, and land in the centre. Watch out for the new streetlights on approach.
en\u001f14.10.2003, Berndt Oberhauser: A: 14 October 2003, Berndt Oberhauser: A lifeline if the escape route from the Gesäuse to Trieben is no longer possible; a short hay meadow at the head of the Johnsbach basin, narrowing towards the end; electric fences may be present! Approach from the west is clear, but there is a wooden fence; a gentle incline to the east (5%), a track crosses halfway along (near the houses; can be driven over, shown in red); the northern section next to the road is flat; towards the south-east, the meadow rises steeply (may be helpful when braking); photo taken from the east towards Admonter Reichenstein, with the track shown in red.
en\u001f14.10.2003, Florian Forcher: Der: 14 October 2003, Florian Forcher: The path in the middle is currently very flat; if necessary, it could be traversed slowly towards the south-east. The upper meadow is approx. 230–250 m long, very flat and rises slightly. A power line crosses the lower meadow (see photo); watch out for the lee side after the patch of woodland on the southern edge. The approach should also be from the north-west to the south-east, particularly in the event of a strong valley wind.
en\u001f14.10.2003, Florian Forcher: Es: 14 October 2003, Florian Forcher: It’s only a second-best option, but better than nothing. This area between Alpl and Vorau is very, very difficult to land in; better to land in a cornfield (Wenigzell) than risk a guaranteed crash. PS: Anyone who reaches the meadow near Pacher at an altitude of approx. 1,150 m should also be able to make it to Wenigzell – EXCEPT when in the lee of a north-westerly wind!! In a north-westerly wind, I would advise against this landing option, as a reasonably safe landing is only possible to the east. There is a slight slope towards the south-east. To the south-east, right at the edge of the picture, a ‘Stipfl’ (irrigation connection) can be seen here.
en\u001f14.12.2022, Klaus-Dieter Degen:: 14 December 2022, Klaus-Dieter Degen: The northern meadow has currently been ploughed (December 2022). Information regarding its future use in spring 2023 is strongly recommended!
en\u001f15.02.2021, Robert Zinnecker: We: 15 February 2021, Robert Zinnecker: To the west of the old meadow is a longer meadow, but the approach is over the motorway.
en\u001f15.03.2021, Alexander Keupp: Akt: 15 March 2021, Alexander Keupp: Current meadow 3.5 km to the south: Several meadows south of Oberried, running parallel to the Ötztaler Ache. Photos from July 2020. Approaching from the south, looking towards the church on the hill (see photo 2). A power line runs parallel to the river and turns off towards Längenfeld.
en\u001f15.03.2021, Alexander Keupp: Das: 15 March 2021, Alexander Keupp: This is the launch site from the 2011 SIP. I visited the site on the ground in the summer of 2019. It is suitable for landing, but the ideal landing area is quite short (approx. 200 m). Be sure to watch out for the post and the bumps in the ground (see diagram). The field shown in the diagram is also used by the local paragliding school.
en\u001f15.03.2021, Benjamin Bachmaier:: 15 March 2021, Benjamin Bachmaier: Alternative to the east: Rises slightly towards the east. Prominent to the south of Hohenburg Castle
15 March 2021, Benjamin Bachmaier: The UL airfield and the surrounding fields are the last possible landing sites to the west of Königsdorf
15 March 2021, Benjamin Bachmaier: The site has been converted into a public park; NOT suitable for landing due to the large number of passers-by. Use the fields to the south.
15 March 2021, Benjamin Bachmaier: Private airfield. Please observe the Oberpfaffenhofen CTR!
15 March 2021, Benjamin Bachmaier: UL airfield in an area with good landing conditions
15 March 2021, Benjamin Bachmaier: An Eastern Alternative
en\u001f15.03.2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: Al: 15 March 2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: Alternative west:. Approach over the thermal baths towards the 25, climbing over a meadow. Approach over a road with normal traffic. Cattle grazing, probably
15 March 2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: Alternative to the west: Unobstructed approach to 32, up the slope. Can, however, be used for agricultural purposes.

15 March 2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: An alternative to the plateau a little further north. Good meadow, BUT: high-voltage power lines to the west of the meadow prevent a cross-wind approach! Another option further south, but also problematic due to the high-voltage power lines!

15 March 2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: Alternative route to the east: To the north of this meadow is a landing site for paragliders with a windsock. Cattle grazing likely! There are a few alternative options, but also plenty of other obstacles (barns, walkers, animals). The terrain rises in the direction of landing 07.
en\u001f15.03.2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: Am: 15 March 2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: Best to land diagonally. That gives you the clearest approach. Wild meadow, probably with grazing livestock
en\u001f15.03.2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: Au: 15 March 2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: Under no circumstances should you land too early. (Slope) Power line running along the northern side of the field. Wild meadow, rising terrain. Several parallel strips, separated by wooden posts. Nassereith North is better
en\u001f15.03.2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: Fr: 15 March 2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: Free approach 09/27. There is a barn at 27, but it doesn’t really get in the way. To the south, a drainage ditch.

15 March 2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: Uncontrolled approach 15/33. 33 is preferable, as the landing strip slopes slightly upwards towards the north. The strip to the east of the road may be longer, but this has not been checked.

15 March 2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: Unobstructed approach from both directions 06/24. No obstacles, but a precision landing is required due to the short runway. Appears to be managed by machinery, so no grazing livestock are to be expected.
15 March 2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: Unobstructed approach. High-voltage lines in the south, a side road in the north. Looks like mechanised farming.
en\u001f15.03.2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: Gu: 15 March 2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: Good meadow; approach only possible from 01 because of high-voltage power lines to the north! Fly over the small power line and land at Quergraben (stream)
en\u001f15.03.2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: La: 15 March 2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: Landing runway 09/27. 09 is better because the field slopes gently towards it. On the approach to 09, there is a railway line, but it can be easily flown over or alongside. On the 28 approach, there is a farm, but you can fly past it to the north.
en\u001f15.03.2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: Nö: 15 March 2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: North of the power line, the terrain slopes slightly to the left and also rises towards the end. To the left of the power line, the line itself is in the way of the final approach. This shortens the meadow considerably! The cross-wind approach is obscured by rocks.
en\u001f15.03.2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: SI: 15 March 2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: SIP Category B. Long trenches!!! Obstacle-free wild meadow; landing only possible in direction 27, with a hill at the end. If approaching from the opposite direction, the approach is too high, making the meadow too short! The ground is rather soft, so a strong braking effect is to be expected.
en\u001f15.03.2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: Se: 15 March 2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: Very short runway – precision landing required. Unobstructed approach. Pedestrians frequently on the runway
en\u001f15.03.2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: Wi: 15 March 2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: Wild meadow with a good approach to 33 (power line). There is a significant bump in the ground near the barn (upwards towards 33), so try to keep slightly to the west if possible. Access via the southern end of the meadow
en\u001f15.03.2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: Zu: 15 March 2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: At the time of the inspection, there were grazing cattle in the meadow. A very uneven meadow.
en\u001f15.03.2021, Gerhard Sindermann:: 15 March 2021, Gerhard Sindermann: I landed there without any problems on 30 July 2020 with my Ka6 CR; see the flight path. The field is fenced off; the farmer wasn’t too keen at first, as the grass was due to be mown very soon. However, he helped me and didn’t cause any trouble. If you land on this field, try to land as far to the right as possible; that way, it’s not far to the exit. Unfortunately, I landed right in the middle. As the meadow is fenced off, there could be animals in it! Length 200m; apart from the fence, there are no obstacles on the approach. The power lines run parallel to the meadows and do not pose an obstacle. It’s also possible to land beyond the fence; there’s a model aeroplane airfield there with a small hut and a windsock, and to the left of that is a wide meadow. You can see everything clearly and measure it out on Google Maps. There are certainly several options; I do not consider the option marked further south on the previous map to be very good. There, you would have to land across the fields. A landing direction of SSE, i.e. out of the valley, is advisable due to the valley wind. Easy access via the Dammweg. On Google Maps (Street View, heading north – you’re looking directly down the approach path), you can see clearly from the road to the left into the grounds; however, these are the meadows that run across the landscape. If you drive as far as just before the petrol station, you’ll see the little cottage at the model aeroplane airfield on the left-hand side of the grounds. Nevertheless, it provides a good insight into the landing conditions.
en\u001f15.03.2021, Jakob Edmaier: Bei L: 15 March 2021, Jakob Edmaier: When landing, look out for the hole in the ground to the south-west. (It is never covered.) Picture on the left: View to the north-east. Picture on the right: View to the south-west.
en\u001f15.03.2021, Manfred Schwegler: A: 15 March 2021, Manfred Schwegler: Approach 25 – over high-voltage power lines and houses (may be critical due to poor flap effect)! The meadow rises steeply towards the west. The valley is somewhat narrow. Valley wind often blows from the north at right angles to the landing direction (in the absence of wider-area winds). A few pegs at the edge. Adjoining former SIP fields are partly dotted with pegs. Alternative: ID 228 Aschau Zillertal (500 m to the north).
en\u001f15.03.2021, Manfred Schwegler: E: 15 March 2021, Manfred Schwegler: Narrow valley. Watch out for valley winds! High-voltage power line to the east, running parallel to the direction of approach. Numerous barns and scattered boundary markers at the edges of the fields. Slightly undulating. As at May 2018.
en\u001f15.03.2021, Manfred Schwegler: F: 15 March 2021, Manfred Schwegler: The field slopes upwards towards the west. Approach over tall trees by the road – but this is not a problem due to the relatively long, sloping field. Land in the front or middle section if possible. The upper section slopes significantly towards the south. During a site inspection in 2018, distinct cracks and isolated holes in the ground were observed, caused by severe drought. There are several other, but shorter, fields in the area.
en\u001f15.03.2021, Manfred Schwegler: L: 15 March 2021, Manfred Schwegler: Landing direction -/22. The field rises steeply towards the south-west. A fairly narrow valley. The approach is very short from a crosswind approach – or, better still, over the hill on the opposite side of the valley. Take care to avoid the telephone line by the road and the fence at the start of the field. Quite undulating in the upper section.

15 March 2021, Manfred Schwegler: Approach from the EAST! Due to a raised road and trees on the eastern edge of the field. Use the cross-field path (which can be driven over if necessary) as a threshold, and watch out for the tree to the right of the field. Otherwise, a clear approach. There are some slightly deeper mouse holes in places. In the event of a strong westerly wind, tall vegetation or if the field is flooded, choose a different field. In that case, however, BEWARE of the many telephone and power lines, particularly in the northern part of the area. In westerly winds, the fields bordering to the west would be suitable, but some of these also have slightly deeper holes. (As of May 2018)
15 March 2021, Manfred Schwegler: Landing direction west, slightly uphill. Watch out for valley winds! Slightly undulating in the upper section.
en\u001f15.03.2021, Manfred Schwegler: T: 15 March 2021, Manfred Schwegler: Watch out for valley winds. Pegs in the meadow are difficult to see. Undulating terrain. The total length of the field is approx. 500 m; however, due to the pegs and the undulating terrain, the area suitable for landing is mainly in the centre and is therefore smaller. An alternative is Achenkirch South, 3 km to the south. As at 3 May 2018.
en\u001f15.03.2021, Manfred Schwegler: Z: 15 March 2021, Manfred Schwegler: Two meadows (1) and (2). Be aware of valley winds; when there are thermals but no wider regional wind, the wind is usually from the north. Watch out for numerous high-voltage power lines, huts, isolated stakes and livestock. 1. South-western meadow, 350 m: When approaching from 07, touch down only after the second row of huts. When approaching from 25, watch out for the high-voltage power line crossing the approach path and, behind the bypass road, touch down only after the cross-path. Pictures from different years show, in some cases, varying use/layout of the meadow (possibly areas with pasture fencing). Choose a suitable landing area. 2. North-eastern meadow, 200 m: A safe approach is only possible from direction 25, via the small crossroad. Take care to avoid the pasture fencing and street lamps on the crossroad! Due to a high-voltage power line running across the south-western part of the meadow, an approach towards 07 would be problematic! (Use Meadow 1 for a landing on 07.)
en\u001f15.03.2021, Mathias Schunk: SIP: 15 March 2021, Mathias Schunk: SIP Category C. The meadow slopes down towards the lake; there is usually a northerly wind.
en\u001f15.03.2021, Mathias Schunk: letz: 15 March 2021, Mathias Schunk: last possible landing site south of the Königsdorf Gliding Centre
15 March 2021, Mathias Schunk: last viable landing site south-east of Königsdorf
en\u001f15.03.2021, Simon Lemmerer: Eine: 15 March 2021, Simon Lemmerer: A long meadow behind the station (and the brothel :-) in Haus im Ennstal. It’s also used by paragliders. The meadow is a bit uneven.
15 March 2021, Simon Lemmerer: A brilliant landing area, almost like an airfield, with good access for trailers
en\u001f15.03.2021, Thomas Münchow: Das: 15 March 2021, Thomas Münchow: The landing area exists for historical reasons. It is the former Flintsbach gliding airfield, but the old runway has since been divided by paths, meaning that the original landing approach is no longer possible. The meadows here are suitable for landing, but are practically within the traffic pattern of the ‘new’ Brannenburg airfield. Provided you still have enough altitude for a reconnaissance circuit, you can also land at Brannenburg. IGC from Condor. I think we should not include this field in the catalogue.
en\u001f15.05.2023, Johannes Schwemmer:: 15 May 2023, Johannes Schwemmer: Visit by car on 12 May 2023 to the Unterwössen Cross-Country Flying Week. Meadow Plan A (blue) is free of obstacles as long as you approach along the main road. Otherwise, there are a few trees, but it is a large, flat meadow of a kind rarely found in the mountains.
15 May 2023, Johannes Schwemmer: Here’s another video of the alternative – the northern, yellow meadow. It’s still just as Roland described. That’s fine too, but we preferred the southern one.
en\u001f15.06.2023, Jürgen Sottung: Heut: 15 June 2023, Jürgen Sottung: Landed here today. There are two meadows side by side. There were cows in the one on the left. I landed in the meadow on the right. They’re separated by an electric fence. Landing facing east went smoothly. At the farm next door, I was immediately offered help, water and coffee.
en\u001f15.08.2023, Klaus-Dieter Degen:: 15 August 2023, Klaus-Dieter Degen: Maize is currently being grown on the northern meadow, so it is no longer accessible. The southern meadow remains accessible.
en\u001f15.09.2025, Jannis Däuble: Maisa: 15 September 2025, Jannis Däuble: Maize cultivation; therefore, depending on the season, the land may be temporarily inaccessible.
en\u001f15.10.2003, Christian Holzner: I: 15 October 2003, Christian Holzner: In Bach there is a place (I think it’s even called the ‘Aviation Centre’) where hang-gliders and paragliders land. In other words, there are wind socks in place and the landing field is certainly at least 250–300 metres long.
en\u001f15.10.2003, Christian Holzner: S: 15 October 2003, Christian Holzner: Very good and even, but there might be some cow pats on it. I had to watch out for cow pats whilst walking along it ;) Werner (Amann) once said: “You only go out into the countryside in the evening anyway, and by then the cattle have gone off”:)
15 October 2003, Christian Holzner: I would avoid Steeg and Holzgau in favour of Bach.
en\u001f15.10.2003, Christian Holzner: Z: 15 October 2003, Christian Holzner: To forget, nothing but barns!

15 October 2003, Christian Holzner: I really cannot recommend Zug. Between the 2,500-metre-high Omeshorn on the south side and the 2,200-metre-high Palmenalpe on the north side, the Zug Valley (1,500 m) is extremely narrow. A landing approach becomes a problem! In these high-alpine regions, it’s not just a question of looking at the meadow, but above all of how you actually get onto that meadow in the first place. What use is a meadow perhaps 150 metres long if you need a helicopter just to make the approach? For me, at any rate, approach options are just as important as the nature of the landing site; a good approach is a prerequisite for a good landing – this applies to the traffic pattern at the airfield, but even more so to a field landing.
en\u001f15.10.2003, Florian Forcher: Kur: 15 October 2003, Florian Forcher: Just before the path (to the east), a power line runs at a height of about 5 metres. There is a second landing option next to the main road: a meadow on a hilltop, though it is rather short (approx. 150–200 m) and really only suitable for a 15-metre wingspan. The landing is also slightly uphill towards the north-west. (Photo to follow). These are only second-rate options, but better than nothing. This area between Alpl and Vorau is very, very difficult to land in; better to land in a cornfield than risk a guaranteed crash.
en\u001f15.10.2003, Florian Forcher: UL: 15 October 2003, Florian Forcher: UL and model aeroplane airfield; in spring it’s usable for the Open Class if necessary, but in summer the maize and the adjacent cereal crops are too tall, so the maximum wingspan allowed is 15 metres.
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: Akt: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: Currently the only option. Frequently used as arable land. The surrounding fields are planted with fruit trees. There is a high fence to the south-west of the field.
15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: Recent photos. There is a permanently installed distribution box at the start of the field.
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: Alt: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: The alternative landing area to the east is in good condition. The blue landing area is currently used as pasture. The main field slopes steeply upwards from the north.
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: Anf: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: Approaches from the west are possible, but restricted due to the length of the runway.
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: Ang: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: The meadow mentioned was NOT inspected. Further good landing options south of the church tower (landing direction 19). Bumps at the start of the field
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: Auf: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: Due to the length of the landing strip and the Category B non-clear approach
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: Ber: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: The field that has already been used lies further west of the field shown here; there is a narrow strip of greenery right next to the barn.
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: Bil: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: Image accompanying the description
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: Bla: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: The blue field can only be landed on from the north and the yellow one only from the east. The meadows are only roughly level at the edge of the woodland; there is a steep slope down towards the houses.
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: Dar: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: The power line shown no longer exists; the south-western section is currently used as pasture.
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: Die: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: The meadows in Oberweiler all slope up towards the village. The preferred landing direction is north.
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: Ehe: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: Former landing field from the Hotzenwald Outlying Landing Fields Catalogue, south-east of the marked landing fields in the valley. (South of the main road at the edge of the village, the field divisions shown on the satellite image are not visible on the ground. The field can be approached from both the east and west. When approaching from the west, watch out for unmarked overhead power lines.)
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: Eig: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: Actual name: Kaltenherberg. Used as pasture for livestock at times. In good condition.
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: Erg: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: Added recent photos.
15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: Update: Recent photograph of the yellow landing area south-west of the main field
15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: Addendum: Photographs of the northern field
15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: Update: The tarmac path running northwards alongside the sports ground is currently occupied by long-term campers.
15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: Update: latest photos
15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: Update: recent photos of the main field

15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: Update: recent photos. The section on the right-hand side of the power line shown in the diagram has been dismantled. This improves landing options to the north of the village. Photos of two different fields
15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: Update: latest image

15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: Addendum: in addition to those already shown, other arable land leading from the village towards the forest can also be used, depending on its current use and vegetation. The fields to the west of the village can also be approached from the west.
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: Fel: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: The pitch was in good condition but a bit bumpy at the start. On the western side of the pitch, there were posts and sheep grazing on the meadow.
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: Fre: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: A friendly owner; he recommends the field directly south of the one currently marked on the map. According to the owner, the field is only used as a paddock in autumn.
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: Gib: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: There are several possible landing sites. + Recent photo taken at dusk from the field further south; the power line shown around the farm was not visible.
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: Gut: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: In good condition – several possibilities. Wind farm to the west of the fields. Update: Recent photos
15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: In good condition. Access is difficult to find: from the swimming pool, follow the green signpost towards Residenz am See.
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: Hau: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: The main field towards the transmitter and the access road are currently used as paddocks
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: Häu: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: The land is frequently used as arable land; the terrain rises steeply towards the north-west.
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: Nac: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: Still a good meadow. Fenced off right by the path – possibly used for grazing. A small model aeroplane flying area to the south of the row of trees
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: Nur: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: Can only be landed from the west. Difficult approach due to the slope on the opposite side. Field divided in the middle by steel posts
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: Sie: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: See Marcus Neubronner’s comment.
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: Str: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: The power line directly above the field is not shown on the map; see Figure 1. Approaching from the north, making it very difficult to touch down after the paved gully beneath the power line
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: UL-: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: The Durrweiler UL airfield is in good condition, with several buzzard hunting perches immediately to the west of the railway line.
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: Wei: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: Further landing areas on both sides – to the left and right – of the tarmac track, currently used as pasture. Model aeroplane airfield with a windsock to the south of the brown field visible in the current satellite image.
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: Wie: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: As can be seen in Marcus Neubronner’s photograph, the airfield is situated north-west of the road. So it is not as shown on the satellite image. Currently used as pasture land. In good condition. Editor’s note: It has now been marked correctly!!!
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: wen: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: poorly maintained farmland
en\u001f15.11.2024, Marven Gründler: Öst: 15 November 2024, Marven Gründler: The eastern field can only be landed on when approaching from direction 20 uphill; the power line shown on the map no longer exists. Approach to the other two fields is only possible from the east! (High forest edge)
en\u001f150/330 Attention moutons mainte: 150/330 Caution: sheep are being kept in a pen across part of the width of the field. May be laid to the east and west.
en\u001f150/330 Passage sous ligne elect: 150/330 Pass under the power line whilst driving across the middle of the field. Keep well to the centre of the field. There is a small stone wall on the east side, near the trees and the post. Access is possible from both directions. When facing south, do not use the start of the field: there is a ditch there.
en\u001f16.01.2004, Mathias Schunk: 2 Mö: 16 January 2004, Mathias Schunk: 2 options (northern field: landing direction 270° only), tall trees on the eastern edge of both fields, southern field slopes down at both ends (“summit at 2/3 of the way along. landing direction 090°)
en\u001f16.02.2024, Herbert Novak: Kontr: 16 February 2024, Herbert Novak: Review 16 February 2024: still a good option
en\u001f16.03.2009, Christoph Lhota: ist: 16 March 2009, Christoph Lhota: Can be landed as originally described in SIP, but with the light guide in the third third and the post in the second third!
en\u001f16.03.2009, Gerhard Windischbaue: 16 March 2009, Gerhard Windischbauer: As part of my preparations for the 2006 Silver Challenge, I flew with two other novice cross-country pilots through the entire Palten-Liesing Valley and the Enns Valley. We visited all the designated landing meadows in this area and assessed them in relation to our current flying ability. We noticed that the Riesenwiese in Altenmarkt, near Schifabrik, is marked as dangerous. However, during our visit on 14 May 2006, the meadow itself was perfectly suitable for landing and, in terms of terrain and layout, was exactly as shown in the photo; it is, however, probably grazed sporadically (cow pats present). On 22 May, I flew over the meadow myself and found it free of cattle.
en\u001f16.03.2024, Manuel Valenta: FSW: 16 March 2024, Manuel Valenta: FSW Landewiesentour 2024: everything’s fine, as described
16 March 2024, Manuel Valenta: FSW Landewiesentour 2024: as described, but the surrounding country meadows are definitely to be preferred
16 March 2024, Manuel Valenta: FSW Landewiesentour: everything’s fine, as described
en\u001f16.03.2025, Benedikt Bursian: La: 16 March 2025, Benedikt Bursian: Landed on 5 September 2024; the meadow remains unchanged. Used as a landing site by paragliders. [ WeGlide flight ]
16 March 2025, Benedikt Bursian: Landed on 30 July 2024. Still going strong. [ WeGlide flight ]
en\u001f16.07.2004, Markus Kienzl: Somme: 16 July 2004, Markus Kienzl: Summer 2004 – the northern part of this meadow has turned into a cornfield.
en\u001f16.07.2021, Albert Kiessling: Di: 16 July 2021, Albert Kiessling: The coordinates and the location are incorrect! Correct: N 48:13:40 E 008:32:05
16 July 2021, Albert Kiessling: The meadows at Oberweiler and Aichhalden have been mixed up!
en\u001f16.10.2003, Erwin Kobermann: Ack: 16 October 2003, Erwin Kobermann: Plough furrows at an angle to the recommended direction of travel
en\u001f16.10.2003, Erwin Kobermann: Ein: 16 October 2003, Erwin Kobermann: Some tracks with tall maize. In between, however, fields with vegetation <15 cm
en\u001f16.10.2023, Peter Stierlen: Wies: 16 October 2023, Peter Stierlen: Meadow 4 km south-west of Malmsheim Airfield for tight final approaches from the south. Approach southwards, past the radio mast. Caution – the north-eastern section slopes steeply down towards the north. The landing area rises by approx. 9 metres from west to east. Not yet required ;-)
en\u001f160/340 Deniveles possibles en l: 160/340 Possible elevation changes at the plot boundary. Landing is recommended towards the south and in the flattest part to the west of the area.
en\u001f165/345 Atterro sur axe parallel: 165/345 Land on a line parallel to the A-road. Touch down and roll across the width of a single field.
en\u001f17.02.2021, Christoph Dolainsky:: 17 February 2021, Christoph Dolainsky: New Scharnitz bypass runs right through the old field
17 February 2021, Christoph Dolainsky: New Scharnitz bypass runs right through the old field
en\u001f17.02.2021, Christoph Jütte: Lan: 17 February 2021, Christoph Jütte: Landing direction instead of 80. 08!
en\u001f17.02.2021, David Richter-Trumme: 17 February 2021, David Richter-Trummer: I visited the Almseewiese again in September 2020; it’s still not an easy landing spot, but it’s certainly possible to land there from the lake.

17 February 2021, David Richter-Trummer: I visited the meadow again in September. It’s still easy to land there from the south-west, but the course of the road has changed, making the meadow narrower than before.
en\u001f17.02.2021, Konrad Mahlknecht: D: 17 February 2021, Konrad Mahlknecht: The meadow is still a good place to land. The photos show the current situation. I landed here in mid-2020 with our B4. The grass was about knee-high. The farmer doesn’t let it grow any taller than that; the grass was mown a week later. Incidentally, he’d be delighted to receive a good bottle of wine.
17 February 2021, Konrad Mahlknecht: This is still a good place to land. Parachute jumps are still taking place on the site.
en\u001f17.02.2021, Manuel Valenta: Wies: 17 February 2021, Manuel Valenta: Wiese is fine, as of March 2020.
en\u001f17.02.2021, Matthias Schündehütt: 17 February 2021, Matthias Schündehütte: Unterwössen has an asphalt runway measuring 800/600 m, with landing directions 24/06. The most common landing direction is 06 (> 90%). The airfield frequency is 131.115 MHz. The airfield elevation is 555 m / 1,820 ft
en\u001f17.02.2021, Michael Derflinger:: 17 February 2021, Michael Derflinger: Field number 3 is now a pick-your-own raspberry patch. The raspberries are growing up along taut wires. This means that only fields 1 and 2 are still available.
en\u001f17.02.2021, Paul Bernhard: 3 Opt: 17 February 2021, Paul Bernhard: 3 options, good access.
en\u001f17.02.2021, Ulrik Beutter: Hallo: 17 February 2021, Ulrik Beutter: Hello. First of all, many thanks for publishing the catalogue. I have a question regarding the landing direction: landings from the south and from the west are possible, but the landing direction is given as -/27. Wouldn’t 09/- and 18/- be more appropriate? Best regards, Ulrik
en\u001f17.02.2021, Wolfgang Frenzel: Fl: 17 February 2021, Wolfgang Frenzel: The airfield is generally open from April to October. Landing direction 330, not 320. Grass runway: 350m long, 30m wide. Landings must be made exclusively in direction 330. The airfield rises by approx. 30m towards the north. Take-offs must be made exclusively in direction 150 (tow-in / self-launch) or in direction 240 (when using a winch). Do not touch down before the landing threshold (asphalt cross-runway).
en\u001f17.03.2004, Thomas Keuschnig: St: 17 March 2004, Thomas Keuschnig: Standard landing site in the Gail Valley, as one of the four areas always has suitable vegetation. The approach is straightforward.
en\u001f17.05.2022, Peter Winkler: Am we: 17 May 2022, Peter Winkler: At the western end of the Murau Ost meadow, a new industrial estate is being built, directly adjacent to the existing ones. However, in my view, the meadow will remain usable.
en\u001f17.06.2021, Hans Trautenberg: Al: 17 June 2021, Hans Trautenberg: Everything’s still fine.
en\u001f17.06.2021, Hans Trautenberg: Di: 17 June 2021, Hans Trautenberg: The meadows to the north, closer to the road, also look very good from the air; there’s even a windsock at the mountain rescue hut there. But these meadows have bumps in the ground as high as a man.
en\u001f17.06.2021, Hans Trautenberg: Es: 17 June 2021, Hans Trautenberg: It is an outdoor airfield where several motorised aircraft are stationed.
en\u001f17.06.2024, Christian Prey: Vom: 17 June 2024, Christian Prey: Viewed from the ground and from the air in June 2024. The meadow is still looking great.
en\u001f17.07.2021, Albert Kiessling: De: 17 July 2021, Albert Kiessling: The town isn’t called Fuhrtwangen – it’s called Furtwangen!
en\u001f17.07.2021, Albert Kiessling: Dr: 17 July 2021, Albert Kiessling: Hang-gliding site, official website: www.ortenauer-dgf.de. Official coordinates: N 48:26:02 E 007:58:15. Winch-launch operations!
en\u001f17.07.2021, Albert Kiessling: Or: 17 July 2021, Albert Kiessling: The place is called Heselbach, not Hesselbach!
en\u001f17.07.2021, Marcus Neubronner: A: 17 July 2021, Marcus Neubronner: All details are included in the PDF. An absolutely trouble-free landing area. It is preferable to land on the 36, due to the slightly sloping terrain. Please note: This is a paramotor flying site! It is therefore mandatory to always announce your landing by radio in advance (even if no one is visible on the field), on 122.555. After landing, please contact the owner, Christoph Nägele, on 0049 151 1078 1915!
en\u001f17.07.2021, Marcus Neubronner: D: 17 July 2021, Marcus Neubronner: The Wehr 2 landing field is situated on the Dinkelberg, to the west and above Wehr.
en\u001f17.07.2021, Paul Bernhard: Alte: 17 July 2021, Paul Bernhard: Old northern meadow built over with houses. The first southern field is approx. 110 metres long; the second field is approx. 330 metres long. Small copses between the fields. Unobstructed approach possible from the north and south. There is a high-voltage power line to the east, so an approach from the west is not possible.
en\u001f17.07.2021, Simon Lemmerer: Die: 17 July 2021, Simon Lemmerer: The field near Eisenerz is definitely suitable for landing smaller-span gliders in an emergency. The field right next to Eisenerz is no longer suitable for landing. The problem here is the valley wind, which usually comes from the west. However, you can roll out about 40 metres further south of the car wash.
en\u001f17.08.2022, Rositsa Ivanova: Die: 17 August 2022, Rositsa Ivanova: The overhead cable has been removed and currently ends to the side of the meadow, so it is not in the way during the final approach. According to the owner, the meadow is also used by paragliders from time to time.
en\u001f17.12.2021, Simon Lemmerer: Supe: 17 December 2021, Simon Lemmerer: Great meadows near Liezen; you can land from north to south, or even across from west to east, as the valley wind in the Ennstal often comes from the east. Good access by car from the road.
en\u001f18.02.2004, Reinhold Ortner: Meh: 18 February 2004, Reinhold Ortner: Several fields, different crops. Located to the west or east, with approaches from the north or south; above this lies Airspace D (SRAI Graz) from 4,000 ft to 7,000 ft; the owner of the northernmost field is August Pölsler, a member of the Graz Gliding Union.
18 February 2004, Reinhold Ortner: Several fields, varying crops. Location: east; approach from the north (high-voltage power line to the south!). Airspace D (SRAI Graz) above, 4,000 ft to 7,000 ft.
en\u001f18.02.2004, Reinhold Ortner: meh: 18 February 2004, Reinhold Ortner: several fields in front of the abbey. Various crops. Located to the south; approach from the east over the sports ground. Windsock at the helipad near Hörgas Regional Hospital. Above this, Airspace D (SRAI Graz) from 4,000 ft to 7,000 ft.
en\u001f18.02.2021, Helmut Zeferer: Auße: 18 February 2021, Helmut Zeferer: The ‘Außenlandfeld 2’ model aeroplane airfield in Urreiting no longer exists; it is now a golf course.
en\u001f18.02.2021, Reinhold Ortner: Gle: 18 February 2021, Reinhold Ortner: Unchanged, meadow OK.
en\u001f18.02.2026, Reinhold Ortner: Fab: 18 February 2026, Reinhold Ortner: The factory isn’t too high. The meadow is still more than enough
en\u001f18.03.2004, Thomas Keuschnig: Di: 18 March 2004, Thomas Keuschnig: The field is larger than the one in the village itself. There is a windsock (used by hang-gliders as a landing area). There is a second landing field to the south of this (west of the campsite), but be careful: there are some fence posts!
en\u001f18.07.2021, Manfred Schwegler: N: 18 July 2021, Manfred Schwegler: Only if you cannot reach Flp Nötsch! 15 July 2021: The meadow was in good condition, freshly mown, during the site inspection. A solid steel pipe stands in the meadow approximately 220 metres from the western edge and approximately 90 metres from the southern edge, overgrown with tall grass. There are also a few smaller wooden posts scattered about. An approx. 50m-wide strip in the southern section, running parallel to the motorway, would probably be the safest landing area at present to avoid the post. With an easterly wind and aircraft with good flap performance, the approach could possibly also be made from the west over the tall trees, given the length of the meadow (approx. 500 metres).
en\u001f18.07.2023, Andrea Kellerer: Lie: 18 July 2023, Andrea Kellerer: Dear Cross-Country Flight Team, On the return flight from Niederöblarn to Unterwössen yesterday (11 July 23), I had to land on a meadow not officially listed, situated immediately to the east of the listed meadow at St. Johann im Pongau. As the tall grass on the meadow north of the football pitch apparently made a landing there unappealing, I opted for the meadow east of the railway line. Due to the prevailing valley wind, I approached from the south, which was only slightly disrupted by a construction crane on the other side of the river. Otherwise, the recently mown field seemed much more inviting. I flew just over the crane (on the right in the picture), but this didn’t interfere with the final approach. The windsock protruding from the tree is clearly visible even from a considerable height thanks to the colour contrast. I imagine that an approach from the north would also work, but you must definitely approach from the east of the high-voltage power line where it bends. In the southern part, the meadow is a bit bumpy, but otherwise – apart from some very slight, long, drawn-out undulations – it’s absolutely no problem. Best regards and many thanks for your efforts – knowing about landing options is invaluable! Andrea.
en\u001f19.02.2021, Reinhold Ortner: Lei: 19 February 2021, Reinhold Ortner: Power lines are clearly visible on the ground. This is not a problem when approaching from the west
en\u001f19.03.2023, Michael Henökl: Agra: 19 March 2023, Michael Henökl: Agricultural land, increasing slightly towards the north-eastern end. 2 trees to the south of the field
en\u001f19.04.2024, Gregor Waldstein: Fo: 19 April 2024, Gregor Waldstein: Photos relating to this
en\u001f19.04.2025, Andreas Riedmüller:: 19 April 2025, Andreas Riedmüller: The sports ground has been levelled.
en\u001f19.04.2025, Michael Buchthal: 23: 19 April 2025, Michael Buchthal: 23 March 2025 – Post-winter inspection: condition remains good. Use is limited for gliders with a larger wingspan due to the trees and the cross-slope.
en\u001f19.04.2025, Michael Buchthal: Be: 19 April 2025, Michael Buchthal: Site visit on 6 April 2025: It was not possible to assess during the site visit how much space there is in the adjacent areas in summer and when the site is operating at full capacity. Often, for hang-gliders and paragliders, only the 15-metre-wide runway is mowed.

19 April 2025, Michael Buchthal: Site visit on 6 April 2025: Photos 1 and 2 show the alternatives in front of the landing area; photos 3–5 show the landing area looking NNW; photo 6 shows the view towards SSO.

19 April 2025, Michael Buchthal: Site visit on 6 April 2025: Apart from the length of the field and the nearby power line, it is a decent landing site. In my view, the landing option shown on the satellite image in the Kinzig floodplain behind the embankment is not a viable alternative due to the bushes right on the bank and the limited width of the area.

19 April 2025, Michael Buchthal: Site visit on 6 April 2025: The illustrated approach direction is from SSW. Due to the tall trees immediately in front of the field and a gentle slope of just 280 metres in this direction, this is rather difficult; the approach from NNE, with the slope leading up to it, is fine.

19 April 2025, Michael Buchthal: Site visit on 6 April 2025: The marked landing area must have been selected solely on the basis of a satellite image. During an on-site inspection, it would never occur to anyone that this depression, with cross-slopes exceeding 45° and no clear approach path, could be considered a landing site (Figures 1–4), let alone a Category A one!!! The field at the top of the hill is accessible from both directions, much like an aircraft carrier, but despite its length of approximately 250 metres, it is not a safe landing option due to undulating terrain and possible lee effects in stronger winds (Pictures 5–8). However, in an emergency, it offers an alternative to attempting a landing in the Kinzig Valley, which is very narrow in this area.

19 April 2025, Michael Buchthal: Site visit on 6 April 2025: The marked landing area must have been selected on the basis of satellite images without an on-site inspection. With a cross-slope of approx. 45°, it has never been suitable for gliders to land on (Figures 2 and 3). The landing site from my 1996 catalogue of out-of-field landing sites was located down by the road (marked in red, Figures 4), and, due to the taller trees on the south-western side and a power line in the north-east, is now only suitable as an emergency landing site. The only option I can see here are the two meadows at the top of the ridge; however, at 200 metres and with an approach across the valley, they too can only serve as a stopgap solution (Figures 5 and 6).

19 April 2025, Michael Buchthal: Site visit on 6 April 2025: The main field at the top of the hill is short but in good condition (Photos 1–3). The approach from the east is completely clear (Photos 4 and 5). The eastern field is level for only about 140 metres at the western end, between the hedges. The terrain then slopes steeply downwards towards the east.

19 April 2025, Michael Buchthal: Site visit on 6 April 2025: Due to the high-voltage power line to the west, the northern field can only be approached sensibly from the east or north-east (Figures 1 and 2). The main field rises towards the village and has a slight cross-slope to the north (Figures 3–6).
19 April 2025, Michael Buchthal: Site visit on 23 March 2025
19 April 2025, Michael Buchthal: Site visit on 23 March 2025; the area is currently slightly reduced in width due to the greenhouses.

19 April 2025, Michael Buchthal: Inspection on 23 March 2025: Previously, inspections had only ever been carried out from the ground in spring or autumn. At those times, there were always several alternatives, such as the current option to land alongside the tarmac path to the south-west (Figures 2 and 3).

19 April 2025, Michael Buchthal: Site visit on 23 March 2025: Wooden posts are visible in the landing field (Figures 1+2); alternatively, the narrower field (approx. 25m wide) between the power line and the stream can also be used (Figures 3+4). There were no posts here. Due to the approach being over and alongside the power line and the one-sided landing direction (only upstream), this is not a Category A site for me.

19 April 2025, Michael Buchthal: Site visit on 23 March 2025: Since the construction of the bypass, the field can now only be approached from the steeper section, and no longer from the northern section directly at the edge of the woodland. The approach should be made in a slight arc towards the north so that, whilst taxiing, the wings can be kept parallel to the ground in a curve.

19 April 2025, Michael Buchthal: Inspection of the meadow in front of the wind turbines on 23 March, and the main field on 6 April 2025: The meadow in front of the wind turbines can be approached either directly from the east across the road (Figure 2), or across open terrain from the north-east (Figure 5) or south (Figure 3). The area measuring 250 x 220 m should be sufficiently large given the unobstructed approach; the north-western areas are also suitable for landing (Figure 5). As can be seen in the overview, the main field (Figures 6–9) is divided into two sections; crossing the boundary should be avoided where possible. The fact that this landing area is immediately bordered by the adjacent woods makes the approach more challenging for gliders with a larger wingspan. The path along the north-eastern threshold is frequently used.

19 April 2025, Michael Buchthal: Inspection of the field running between the power line and the tarmac path on 23 March; the main field shown in the illustration was inspected on 6 April 2025: The alternative shown, running in a south-west to north-east direction, does not appear to me to be a viable option due to the obstacles posed by the woodland and the power line.
