The elongated field (Pictures 1–3) has no breaks in the terrain; the area is therefore usable throughout, provided the vegetation is uniform. At the time of the site visit, the main field (Figures 4–8) was criss-crossed with iron rods.

19 April 2025, Michael Buchthal: Site visit south of the Kinzig on 23 March, north of the Kinzig on 6 April 2025: Regardless of the length of the fields, the approach is very challenging in the Kinzig Valley, which is already quite narrow at this point. Picture 1: south of the Kinzig; Picture 2: north of the Kinzig.

19 April 2025, Michael Buchthal: Site visit on 23 March 2025: Condition good; as Ralf Fischer has already noted, the approach is very difficult due to the narrow valley and the tall trees. Personally, I would prefer the field near the sewage treatment works in the rear Renchtal valley near Ramsbach (3rd picture, looking down the valley, approximately NNW). There, the valley is already slightly wider and the approach is somewhat better.
en\u001f19.04.2025, Michael Buchthal: Sp: 19 April 2025, Michael Buchthal: Conditions later in the season are still difficult to assess at this time of year; apart from the overhead power line, there are good approach areas. The Bohlsbach Ost field, west of the bridge over the railway line, is an alternative in this area.
en\u001f19.05.2021, Volker Davids: anbei: 19 May 2021, Volker Davids: Attached are a few photos of the last off-airport landing there on 14 May 2021. It really is a perfect place to land!
en\u001f19.06.2022, Reinhold Vogel: Ansi: 19 June 2022, Reinhold Vogel: Perch (bird of prey pole) in the middle of the field. It’s best to land to the north of it.
en\u001f19.06.2023, Ladislav Jozsa: At t: 19.06.2023, Ladislav Jozsa: At the time of the landing possibly the only meadow with cut grass. Wind was pretty much calm. You have to land out towards the village. The slope of the terrain is upwards. Make sure you have 10km/h landing speed more than normal and aim for the very beginning of the meadow, just past the road south of the meadow. It's not an easy landing - you have a road and houses 200m in front of you - but uphill which is more steep towards the road in the north. Try to not take this field in account unless you have to, there's a significant risk of damaging your glider. There are very nice and helpful people around. The vice mayor house is just around the corner and he helped me a lot while I was waiting for transport back. [ WeGlide Flug ]
en\u001f19.07.2021, Manfred Schwegler: A: 19 July 2021, Manfred Schwegler: The approach is straightforward. During a site visit on 16 July 2021, the north-western meadow north of the tree was suitable for landing, as was the south-eastern meadow (marked with a dotted line in the old SIP catalogue, and in yellow in the new one). The rest was planted with maize or marsh grass – and very damp there. A standard landing meadow in the Gail Valley, as one of the three areas is likely to have suitable vegetation most of the time.
en\u001f19.07.2023, Hans Trautenberg: Is: 19 July 2023, Hans Trautenberg: It’s still good.
en\u001f20.02.2021, Raphael Ladner: Abgr: 20 February 2021, Raphael Ladner: Demarcation of several strips of meadow by posts in the meadow (spaced out in the direction of flight). On approach, trees and a power line must be flown over. (As at February 2021)
en\u001f20.02.2021, Thomas Wettemann: Gr: 20 February 2021, Thomas Wettemann: Large flat area along the St2073 between villages, above the Mangfall – easy to land on, unobstructed approaches. Landing direction depends on wind direction; no significant gradient. Several patches of meadow at least 300 metres long. Choice of meadow depends on how it is managed. Eastern meadows are in better condition. Southern sections are permanently grazed by livestock.
en\u001f20.07.2021, Manfred Schwegler: E: 20 July 2021, Manfred Schwegler: Addendum: Length 250 m, width approx. 45 m.
en\u001f20.07.2021, Manfred Schwegler: L: 20 July 2021, Manfred Schwegler: The only area suitable for landing is the meadow west of the path leading up to the farm! The approach is from the lake, over tall trees and streetlights along the road, making it relatively short. However, the meadow slopes upwards from the lake. Usable only as far as the small hut to the right of the farm. Partly slightly undulating. All other meadows contain pasture fences, stakes or other obstacles. Site inspection 15 July 2021.

20 July 2021, Manfred Schwegler: Approach heading only 29 due to a telephone line to the west. Approach over tall trees. Watch out for the high-voltage power line to the south. During a site inspection on 16 July 2021, the southern meadow was partly planted with tall maize. The landing strip, approximately 250 metres long, was only about 40 metres wide at the start, narrowing to 25 metres. The landing threshold is ‘marked’ by a manure heap approx. 1 m high. After the harvest and in the spring, the field is likely to be easier to land on again. The northern meadow was overgrown with tall grass but was still suitable for landing; there were a few tyre tracks across it (which can be driven over, albeit with a bit of a bump). There is a single stake at the start of the meadow, so do not touch down too far in front of the tree standing alone on the embankment (approx. 30 metres before it is fine). There is a usable length of approx. 220 metres up to the telephone line. The telephone line can be crossed if necessary.
en\u001f20.07.2022, Michael Derflinger:: 20 July 2022, Michael Derflinger: You could include a link to the video, or at least add the title: ‘Glider outlands in an Alpine Valley’. By: Wojciech Wojtaczka.
en\u001f20.10.2023, Peter Stierlen: Feld: 20 October 2023, Peter Stierlen: Field 1 = centre of the blue area. Field 2 = to the south of it. Field 3 = to the north. Field 4 = to the north and to the right of the railway line. To the east of fields 1, 2 and 3 runs a railway line with overhead lines. Between the northern and central meadows is an abrupt rise in the terrain with a height difference of approximately 0.5 m. In the southern part of the middle field, there is a ditch which also cannot be crossed. The southern field is slightly shorter due to the vegetation on its southern edge. Plan the approach with gliders over a wider area than shown on the former microlight approach chart. Note for the admin only! I have attached the images with and without captions. Please modify as required. I took the approach map over 30 years ago ;-)
en\u001f21.02.2021, Andreas Hudler: Lage: 21 February 2021, Andreas Hudler: Location: West of Tösens Tschupbach, north of the Inn and the main road. Several large areas to choose from; just watch out for a small stone wall near the road. Both landing directions are possible, with unobstructed approaches; other meadows are also possible, with only slight drops between them. Quality: very good
en\u001f21.02.2021, Andreas Hudler: Nord: 21 February 2021, Andreas Hudler: North-east of Landeck, in the bend of the Inn. Small patches of land, alternating land use, 2 high-voltage power lines causing interference. The Venet cable car runs very close to the slope but does not cause any hindrance. Landing direction: west. Approach coming from Landeck along the southern slope, left-hand turn over the Venet cable car valley station, touchdown, then roll past the tree on the left. Landing direction east: Approach coming from Landeck along the northern slope, right-hand turn over the high-voltage pylon, short and steep descent, touchdown to the right of the tree. Quality: difficult, but OK
en\u001f21.02.2021, Andreas Hudler: Nörd: 21 February 2021, Andreas Hudler: North of Prutz, east of the Inn and the main road. Large area sloping gently northwards. Both landing directions possible, north preferred: terrain not a factor, keep to the right, close to the path. Landing direction south: in mountain winds, approach towards the pylons of the cross-country power line. Fly low to the left past the pylon and touch down behind it. Quality: very good. Note: Watch out for vegetation and choose the best part of the meadow.
en\u001f21.02.2021, Andreas Hudler: Südl: 21 February 2021, Andreas Hudler: South of Imst in the industrial estate near the motorway exit. An eastbound landing is standard and has been tried and tested many times, on both fields. A westbound landing is also fine, but there is less space for manoeuvring and the approach. Quality: good and tried and tested

21 February 2021, Andreas Hudler: South of Nauders, heading towards the Reschen Pass. East of the main road and the stream. South of the overhead power line. Negligible gradient, slopes slightly towards the stream at the side. Landing direction north is fine. (Landing direction south is very tricky; if necessary, fly under the power line and glide for a further 200 metres). Quality: moderate. Very damp meadow with wet ditches, which should not be crossed. Differences in the colour of the vegetation indicate the location of the ditches!
en\u001f21.02.2021, Andreas Hudler: Östl: 21 February 2021, Andreas Hudler: East of Haiming, halfway to Silz, north of the main road: Several open areas; hardly any breaks between the meadows, so no problem with overrunning. Choose suitable grassy areas depending on current usage! Both landing directions are possible; easy to approach, though the usable area may be short.

21 February 2021, Andreas Hudler: East of the village of Arzl i.P. A long, flat area, alternating between grass and cultivated land. Choose wisely! Both landing directions are possible, and it’s easy to fly in. Note: About 2 km away from the tried-and-tested meadow in the Imst industrial estate and approx. 160 m higher.
en\u001f21.02.2021, Christoph Epe: Die v: 21 February 2021, Christoph Epe: The remaining meadow behind the horse fence described earlier was, today, 21 February 2021, divided in the middle by two portable pasture fences, making it very difficult to land and leaving only a very short landing area on either the front or back half.
en\u001f21.02.2021, Christoph Epe: Genau: 21 February 2021, Christoph Epe: Exactly as previously described (2009) in the text, photos and map. The north–south route along the 19th course usually has a tailwind and can therefore be very short due to the trees at the threshold. The 14th course is a better option.
en\u001f21.02.2021, Christoph Epe: Hier: 21 February 2021, Christoph Epe: Here is a second photo of the pasture fences described earlier, running right through the middle of the field.
en\u001f21.02.2021, Christoph Epe: Hundh: 21 February 2021, Christoph Epe: Hundham 1 / North, WARNING: A grab bar at the ‘threshold’, 30 metres past the crossroads where the landing field begins, 40 metres from the road, 5 metres high! So approach from a slightly higher altitude and land towards the rear of the field. Further poles and posts can be avoided by landing next to the road, as shown on the map.
21 February 2021, Christoph Epe: Hundham 1 / North: several handrails and posts in the middle. Land parallel to the road, close by.
21 February 2021, Christoph Epe: Hundham 2 / South: no problems, OK, as per the old description.
en\u001f21.02.2021, Christoph Epe: Nach: 21 February 2021, Christoph Epe: About halfway along the route, there is a two-metre-high post on the right-hand side in the direction of travel, standing about 15 metres from the edge of the field. If you keep to the left, there’s no problem.
en\u001f21.02.2021, Thomas Wettemann: Nu: 21 February 2021, Thomas Wettemann: Use by the horse farm has intensified; fields further subdivided by fences! No reliable landing area – used as a paddock. Landing in short sections may still be possible.
en\u001f21.02.2021, Thomas Wettemann: Ob: 21 February 2021, Thomas Wettemann: Terrain: Meadows – slightly undulating. Ruts in the western section and smaller ones along the central meadow path. Choose a landing zone depending on how the land is used. Best ground conditions run parallel to the central meadow path, to the west and east. Landing possible on both sides; no utility lines.
en\u001f21.03.2025, Andreas Weitz: Besic: 21 March 2025, Andreas Weitz: Site visit March 2025. The southern, shorter meadow is very flat compared to the others. There is a fence along the eastern path; at the farmyard south of the meadow there are two individual posts; and along the western edge there are some individual posts. The farmer seems to mow his meadows in different ways from time to time, which means the meadows can sometimes be divided (see satellite images from recent years). Further south, directly by the southern path – where landings have already taken place – there are significantly more bumps in the ground, some of which are very high.

21 March 2025, Andreas Weitz: Site visit March 2025. The southern, shorter meadow is very flat compared to the others. There is a fence along the eastern path; two individual posts stand in the farmyard to the south of the meadow; and there are some individual posts along the western edge. An approach to the more southerly meadow is possible from both directions. When landing towards the west, you must fly over the fence by the path; when landing towards the east, you should also avoid flying too low due to the occasional single posts. Take care also because of the trees. The farmer seems to mow his meadows in different patterns from time to time, which can sometimes split the meadows in two (see satellite images from recent years). Further south, directly by the southern path – where landings have already taken place – there are significantly more bumps, some of which are very high.
en\u001f21.04.2025, Christian Hynek: All: 21 April 2025, Christian Hynek: Everything as usual! Photo taken from the Myra Falls.
en\u001f21.05.2023, Simon Lemmerer: Auss: 21 May 2023, Simon Lemmerer: Off-field landing after an early take-off today, Top Wiese — there are irrigation channels in the middle, but the last third heading east is the best place to land. Valley winds almost always come from the east!
en\u001f21.07.2021, Matthias Knopf: Ich: 21 July 2021, Matthias Knopf: I was recently in St. Johann/Pongau and had a look at the 100-schilling meadows. Apart from the high-voltage power line to the north (which is well known), unfortunately floodlight masts are also likely to be installed on the southern side. As the meadows are short, you have to start the flare manoeuvre early. This could lead to a collision with one of the floodlight masts (approx. 20 metres high). I believe these were erected later. I wouldn’t want to land here in a two-seater in crosswinds. I prefer the meadow to the right of that, next to the river; according to local residents, a female pilot (name unknown, presumably from Wiener Neustadt) is said to have landed there successfully. Please could you give your assessment and take this into account.
en\u001f21.08.2024, Benedikt Bursian: La: 21 August 2024, Benedikt Bursian: Landing on 2 May 2024 in Föhn conditions, i.e. with the wind blowing out of the valley and landing heading south. High-voltage power lines were visible on approach in this landing direction. In the middle of the meadow is what appears to be a new cowshed; as a result, the roll-out distance is limited in both landing directions due to the curvature of the field. [ WeGlide flight ]

21 August 2024, Benedikt Bursian: Landed on 3 March 2024 in Föhn conditions, i.e. approaching from the west. The meadow is in good condition; the power line that used to cross it is no longer there. Cross-country paths can be crossed at low speed. [ WeGlide flight ]
en\u001f215 Bordure plateau. Risque turb: 215 Plate edge. Risk of turbulence at low altitude.
en\u001f22.03.2021, Christoph Dolainsky:: 22 March 2021, Christoph Dolainsky: Furrows in the meadow, possibly also Ri 12 diagonally against the valley wind.
en\u001f22.03.2024, Gregor Waldstein: Ac: 22 March 2024, Gregor Waldstein: Please note: The field in Viehofen is no longer suitable for glider landings. Excavated material from a nearby hotel construction site has been dumped on the western side of the field. There are still large mounds of earth at the eastern end of the levelled area. The distance from the path to the end of the levelled area is approximately 200 metres.
en\u001f22.04.2024, Valentin Lindlar: UL: 22 April 2024, Valentin Lindlar: UL airfield. Good landing option on frequency 125.365 05/23 520x20m
en\u001f22.10.2003, Christian Hynek: Ach: 22 October 2003, Christian Hynek: Please note that the ‘new’ motorway and the sewage treatment works are missing from the map!!! It is not known to what extent the meadow is still suitable for landing???
en\u001f22.10.2003, Florian Forcher: Da: 22 October 2003, Florian Forcher: Back in 2001, there was still a meadow there; it’s now only half as long – see the new field coming into view – and is actually quite short (an emergency landing area, but still better than the Feistritzgraben); you can only land from the east.
en\u001f23.01.2004, Christian Holzner: O: 23 January 2004, Christian Holzner: OK, if we have to. There’s a post on the eastern field about 100 metres in. To the south (to the right of it when heading in that direction), there’s still enough space to get past it or to land shortly afterwards.
en\u001f23.02.2021, Alfred Federlehner:: 23 February 2021, Alfred Federlehner: The meadow remains unobstructed as per the catalogue, but there are nevertheless some slight changes; I would therefore divide the meadow into the following sections: - The western part, facing the main road, is significantly divided by the farm track (shown as a black line in the catalogue); to the north of the track there is now a maize field, whilst to the south there is still a meadow, though it is quite undulating – see photos! - The eastern part (extending from the arable field) remains a lovely meadow, rising gently towards the east; I would only land here by approaching from the west and heading east (rising terrain). Photos looking east.

23 February 2021, Alfred Federlehner: The meadow remains free of obstacles as per the catalogue. (There is a model aeroplane and paraglider landing area by the small hut on the corner!) Thin wooden stakes, which mark the course of the cross-country ski trail in winter where visible, are being removed again. Photo looking north-west.
23 February 2021, Alfred Federlehner: Meadow exactly as described in the catalogue! Photos looking north

23 February 2021, Alfred Federlehner: Meadow as described in the catalogue, with no obstacles! (However, I would recommend approaching it only from the west (over the row of trees), as the ground rises slightly towards the farmhouse). Photo looking west.
en\u001f23.02.2021, Christian Hynek: Wie: 23 February 2021, Christian Hynek: Wiese is fine!
en\u001f23.02.2021, Flugsport-Wien: Anfl: 23 February 2021, Flugsport-Wien: Approach 12, grain fields; approach across the direction of ploughing. Caution: high-voltage power lines in the Piesting Valley
23 February 2021, Flugsport-Wien: Approach 05/23 SW: L=250m NE: L=200m A barbed-wire fence divides two meadows in Aichlmühl / Eichelmühl
en\u001f23.02.2021, Flugsport-Wien: Aus: 23 February 2021, Flugsport-Wien: 26 pastures with fences along the road, length 300 m. 09 rows of trees, electricity pylons to the west, a dip to the north
en\u001f23.02.2021, Flugsport-Wien: Bäum: 23 February 2021, Flugsport-Wien: Aircraft must fly over the trees in the 05 direction.
en\u001f23.02.2021, Flugsport-Wien: Küb: 23 February 2021, Flugsport-Wien: Küb West: Roll slowly across the centre of the runway
en\u001f23.02.2021, Flugsport-Wien: Schm: 23 February 2021, Flugsport-Wien: Narrow valley, little space for head-on and cross-wind approaches; very difficult to spot when approaching from the east (Preiner Gscheid). Pasture with barbed-wire fencing.
en\u001f23.02.2021, Tomasz Królik: Ich w: 23 February 2021, Tomasz Królik: I was in Flintsbach three weeks ago. The hangar is still standing, but there is no longer a runway there. The fields are suitable for landing. The Rosenheim Aviation Club can confirm this information.
en\u001f23.03.2024, Patrick Misun: Zwing: 23 March 2024, Patrick Misun: Mandatory landing direction 27. Give preference to the southern landing field. There is a large boundary stone with wooden posts between the southern and northern fields. Northern landing field: There are wooden posts in the northern part (upper third) of the landing field. Therefore, use the southern side of the northern landing field.
en\u001f23.04.2022, Jürgen Kartnaller: A: 23 April 2022, Jürgen Kartnaller: Last used on 18 April 2022. Excellent landing spot. As there are posts right in the middle, you should always aim to land on the southern side of the meadow (towards the road). Use the arrows marked on the map for your approach.
en\u001f23.05.2023, Werner Wenger: Heuer: 23 May 2023, Werner Wenger: This year, the meadow is being used for agricultural purposes and is divided into two halves (maize/low vegetation).
en\u001f23.08.2022, Raphael Gramer: Auf: 23 August 2022, Raphael Gramer: Please be aware of the power line on the south-western edge (see photo). At the pylon situated roughly halfway across the meadow, the cables run underground. Landing in the direction of 33 is not advisable due to the residential buildings in front of the runway. It should also be possible to land on the meadow bordering the south-western side, but I was unable to check it for any potential unevenness due to the tall grass at the time.
en\u001f23.10.2008, Christoph Lhota: Um: 23 October 2008, Christoph Lhota: From the air, many meadows around Bad Ischl look as though they would be suitable for landing, but only one is actually suitable.
en\u001f24.02.2004, Herwig Wagner: Das L: 24 February 2004, Herwig Wagner: The Lesachtal is known far beyond Carinthia as an unlandable area riddled with ropes. The Carinthians say that every second glider that lands outside the Lesachtal is written off afterwards. The only – albeit not entirely straightforward – landing option!
en\u001f24.03.2021, Paul Bernhard: neues: 24 March 2021, Paul Bernhard: new aerial photograph, in good condition
en\u001f24.03.2024, Marcus Neubronner: H: 24 March 2024, Marcus Neubronner: Here’s another link to a video of the landing field in Wolfach.
en\u001f24.05.2005, Peter Stackler: Hatt: 24 May 2005, Peter Stackler: Last weekend I had the pleasure of flying cross-country in Prutz/Oberinntal with an ASH 25 fitted with winglets. I also opted for the field listed in the SIP, although I chose the one running parallel to the main road after the village of Faggen (which is an extension of the field you recommended), approaching from south to north, as your field might have been too short. During the crosswind approach, watch out for high-voltage pylons; it’s best to begin the final approach over the houses in Faggen and keep well to the left (close to the main road) as there are hay barns on the right – should the landing run out of distance, it’s possible to glide under the power lines (there’s sufficient lateral clearance), though this wasn’t the case for me. The condition of the field is ideal, with no bumps or ditches; there’s a tarmac road right next to it, and an Esso petrol station for refreshments whilst you wait is about 8 minutes away.
en\u001f24.05.2021, Michael Leonardelli:: 24 May 2021, Michael Leonardelli: Easy to land along the stream. A wide strip has been mown across the middle.
24 May 2021, Michael Leonardelli: Dirt track crossing, not mown

24 May 2021, Michael Leonardelli: There’s a windsock hanging in the eastern part of the meadow. A high-voltage power line crosses it, and another power line runs alongside it – you need to watch out for that one.
24 May 2021, Michael Leonardelli: Not bad at all – there’s a windsock in the north-west corner of the meadow. Flanked on both sides by high-voltage power lines.
en\u001f240/060 Travaux en cours: Imposa: 240/060 Work in progress: Caution! Very tricky fields. Reconnaissance on foot essential. Paragliding activity near field #2. Landings facing east or south. Hay.
en\u001f25.01.2023, Martin Stessl: UL La: 25 January 2023, Martin Stessl: UL landing field. Mowed all year round. A high-voltage power line runs parallel to the runway, 50 metres to the east. Watch out for microlight aircraft.
en\u001f25.01.2023, Martin Stessl: UL-La: 25 January 2023, Martin Stessl: UL landing field. Mowed all year round
25 January 2023, Martin Stessl: UL landing area. Mowed all year round. Watch out for the hangar at the start of runway 09.
en\u001f25.02.2005, Christian Holzner: D: 25 February 2005, Christian Holzner: Both fields are on a slight slope. Consequently, the eastern field can only be approached from the west and the western field only from the east.
en\u001f25.02.2005, Christian Holzner: U: 25 February 2005, Christian Holzner: You must use this landing area, as the other, supposedly good areas are littered with wooden pegs.
en\u001f25.02.2024, Marcus Neubronner: B: 25 February 2024, Marcus Neubronner: Site visit on 25 February 2024. In my view, the meadow south of the main road mentioned is very difficult to use. On approach, there are various power lines to the east, as well as a tree in the middle of the meadow. This makes it difficult for aircraft with a large wingspan (18m+). It might be feasible for a wooden aeroplane or a club-class aircraft with some skill. When approaching from the west, you also have to fly over industrial buildings, so in either case you don’t really have a clear approach. The field on the northern side of the main road is also very challenging, as it is even shorter but significantly wider. If you touch down early, you’ll have about 200 metres to roll out. The approach from west to east is largely unobstructed, with the exception that you have to fly over the main road right at the very end. It’s essential to time your approach so that there isn’t a large coach parked in front of the meadow. All in all, as things stand today, this option seems better to me than the meadow currently listed. I’d feel confident attempting a landing in a Club Class aircraft. With an 18-metre aircraft, I’d only do so in a genuine emergency. So, exercise caution.
en\u001f25.03.2005, Christoph Lhota: Ent: 25 March 2005, Christoph Lhota: Drainage ditches running PERPENDICULAR to the direction of flight, which flow into the main drainage channels!! Give preference to Möderndorf or Tressdorf. (I would not include this meadow in my flight planning)
en\u001f25.03.2005, Robert Tobler: Aufgr: 25 March 2005, Robert Tobler: Given the short length of the field and the presence of buildings on one side of the approach path and the woodland on the other, the field must be classified as Category B.
en\u001f25.03.2005, Robert Tobler: Für d: 25 March 2005, Robert Tobler: In the event that, when approaching Mariazell from the west, you are unable to clear the Zeller Rain, there is a landing field just before it. Hans-Peter Gold has landed there before. Classification B, as it is slightly undulating.
en\u001f25.03.2021, Melchior Hartmann: -: 25 March 2021, Melchior Hartmann: - The runway is somewhat narrow and slopes down towards the east. - The runway bends roughly at the halfway mark. - The airfield is regularly used by microlight helicopters
en\u001f25.06.2026, Benedikt Bursian: La: 25 June 2026, Benedikt Bursian: Landing on 25 June 2026. The field itself is in good condition; the power line is clearly visible on approach. The field slopes down towards the north, so a slightly angled approach is advisable.
en\u001f26.02.2004, Mathias Schunk: Das: 26 February 2004, Mathias Schunk: Unfortunately, the field was already covered in snow, but it looked as though it was criss-crossed by fences or ditches!
en\u001f26.02.2021, Manfred Schwegler: W: 26 February 2021, Manfred Schwegler: The meadow is suitable for landing, subject to the usual restrictions: it slopes down towards the lake; the final approach is over built-up areas; the meadow is short; only use the southern part up to the fence or the row of trees. Be aware of the power line to the north-east. Livestock may be present at times.
en\u001f26.03.2011, Robert F. Tobler: Di: 26 March 2011, Robert F. Tobler: Although the meadow is not particularly wide, it is very long and flat. The power lines to the left and right do not restrict the width of the meadow.
en\u001f26.03.2011, Robert F. Tobler: Ma: 26 March 2011, Robert F. Tobler: On this large meadow, you should land at least 50 metres east of the high-voltage power line, as there are fences in the western part.
en\u001f26.03.2011, Thomas Keuschnig: in: 26 March 2011, Thomas Keuschnig: initial feedback. Pictures from the southern ‘Alternativwiese’!
en\u001f26.06.2011, Christian Hofer: Die: 26 June 2011, Christian Hofer: The field can now be described as unsuitable for glider landings, as strawberry and raspberry fields have been cultivated here for around three years and are fenced off on several occasions between May and September. At present, the field is also only suitable for kites to a limited extent. An alternative can be found about 1.5 km to the east, running parallel to the south runway.
en\u001f26.06.2023, Rositsa Ivanova: Gut: 26 June 2023, Rositsa Ivanova: Good landing site. The meadow consists of several sections. The vegetation can be quite tall in places. Maize was grown on one section of the meadow.
en\u001f26.06.2023, Rositsa Ivanova: Wie: 26 June 2023, Rositsa Ivanova: The meadow is fine. It’s best to land in the middle, as that’s where it’s flattest. It rises slightly from south to north. The southernmost part (50 m) is relatively steep.
en\u001f26.07.2023, Alistair Bruce: Imme: 26 July 2023, Alistair Bruce: Still a good option
en\u001f26.07.2024, Anneliese Hagl: Land: 26 July 2024, Anneliese Hagl: Landed on 25 July 2024. The meadow is in excellent condition, completely flat with no holes or ruts whatsoever. [ WeGlide flight ]
en\u001f26.08.2025, Stefan Schuster: Am: 26 August 2025, Stefan Schuster: Landed the LS4 on 26 August 2025. Approached from the east. The field was in good condition.
en\u001f26.09.2021, Anton Achleitner: Be: 26 September 2021, Anton Achleitner: Confirmed. There is a cattle fence and an earth embankment in the meadow, meaning that the landing strip for approach direction 04 is now only 220 m long and also has a significant gradient in the first quarter. Land on the roadside. May 2021.
en\u001f26.09.2021, Anton Achleitner: Bi: 26 September 2021, Anton Achleitner: Please remove this entirely from the list > Landing is life-threatening > deep holes, extremely uneven, barbed wire in the middle of the meadow, currently used as a cattle pasture, in short. Better to use Langwies or land in the lake. June 2021
en\u001f26.09.2021, Anton Achleitner: Go: 26 September 2021, Anton Achleitner: Gosau – Mittertal Meadow 1: Please remove this entirely from the list, as a large hotel complex has since been built there; the meadow is now very narrow, and there are often people in the area, with a children’s playground located nearby. May 2021. Gosau – Meadow 2: Unchanged and confirmed – good landing option; 330 m of meadow; preferred landing direction 22; unobstructed approach. Please note: there is a thick boundary post in the geographical centre of the field; the side facing the road is completely flat and free of obstacles, whilst the side facing the stream is undulating and not particularly suitable. Wind conditions are often unclear; however, there are several wind vanes in the vicinity. May 2021
en\u001f26.09.2021, Anton Achleitner: Je: 26 September 2021, Anton Achleitner: Accessible depending on the crop and season, but the slope rises steadily > the difference in height is 25 metres. The meadow is currently inaccessible due to tall maize crops. However, there are alternative landing options in the area.
en\u001f26.09.2021, Anton Achleitner: Un: 26 September 2021, Anton Achleitner: Unchanged and confirmed – very good landing spot; well-maintained meadow; on days with thermals, the landing direction is usually 28 July 2021.

26 September 2021, Anton Achleitner: Unchanged and confirmed – good landing spot; on days with thermals, wind from the left – be aware of possible lee conditions from the row of trees on the left. Well-maintained meadow, rising at the far end. Wind vanes at the DIY store/end section. Caution: in the meadow south-east of the house there is an iron crossbar serving as a boundary post. July 2021.

26 September 2021, Anton Achleitner: Unchanged and confirmed – good landing field, rising slightly towards Mitterndorf (27). To the south-east of the meadow, near the ski jump facility, there is a model aeroplane airfield with a windsock. July 2021.

26 September 2021, Anton Achleitner: Unchanged and confirmed – for emergencies only. Mostly strong tailwind from the north (lake). Landing from the south is too short due to power lines. 300 m east of the meadow there is a windsock (paraglider). June 2021

26 September 2021, Anton Achleitner: Unchanged and confirmed. All the meadows slope gently upwards by a total of approx. 25 m. Please note that the houses at the far end are situated approx. 8 m above the starting level of the meadows. The prevailing wind direction is from the north. The dozen or so strips of meadow are all marked out with wooden pegs. Meadow 1 is also suitable for transporting the aircraft away. July 2021
en\u001f26.10.2022, Paul Bernhard: Anflu: 26 October 2022, Paul Bernhard: Approach possible from both sides. High-voltage power line to the north
en\u001f26.10.2022, Paul Bernhard: Gute: 26 October 2022, Paul Bernhard: A good alternative to Murau East. Options to the north and south of the road.
en\u001f27.02.2004, Ulrich Plarre: Ist n: 27 February 2004, Ulrich Plarre: Rises slightly towards the north.
en\u001f27.02.2021, Christian Ossmann: F: 27 February 2021, Christian Ossmann: Frequency: 123.350 MHz. Gliders are requested to fly the western traffic pattern and avoid flying low over the village of Antersberg. Photos:
