en\u001fInfo: Approach over 10m high tre:
Information: Approach over 10m high trees
Surface: Grass Land
Direction: -/24
Tour: 2024
Amended: 06.06.2024
Reported hazards: Electricity and other cables
Feedback:
- 2 June 2024, Jan Lyczywek: Site visit on 1 June 2024. Slightly uphill from east to west. Approach therefore, and due to trees, only from east to west (towards the valley); with a valley headwind, this means a tailwind. Quite a lot has changed compared to Ulrich Plarre’s old photographs: 1. 100 metres past the easternmost edge of the meadow, a track now crosses the meadow at right angles to the landing direction. The track itself would be passable, but during the site visit there was a fence on one side. However, you haven’t actually reached the ground by the time you reach the track. You can treat the new track as a threshold. 2. On the northern edge of the meadow, at the start of this track, there is a newly built barn. Although this makes the meadow appear narrower from the right, it does not otherwise cause any hindrance. 3. The distance from the new track to the substation (called ‘Schober’ by Urs) is exactly 200 metres. However, you can ride past the substation on the left. 4. From the substation to the edge of the woodland, there is a further 100 to 120 metres of overshoot to the edge of the woodland, the westernmost end of the meadow. 5. At the southern edge of the meadow, gravel is either being extracted or used for backfilling (“land filling” according to the signage). In any case, only the northern half of the meadow’s width is usable; the southern half forms a slope descending from south (the southern edge of the woodland) to north, across the direction of flight. Short, narrow, a rather poor B. Inspection video:
- 27 February 2021, Urs Wolfensberger: The meadow is not 400 metres long, but only 290 metres (from Schober to the edge of the wood). Between Schober and the new gravel pit, it is now quite narrow, at around 25 metres. Aerial photograph: geoland.at
en\u001fInfo: Approach over sports field:
Information: Approach over sports field
Surface: Grass Land
Direction: -/36
Tour: 2024
Amended: 27.02.2025
Reported hazards: Electricity and other cables
Feedback:
- 2 February 2025, Philip Heinrich: Brilliant save. The goal was wide open.
- 18 July 2023, Andrea Kellerer: Dear Cross-Country Flight Team, On the return flight from Niederöblarn to Unterwössen yesterday (11 July 23), I had to land on a meadow not officially listed, situated immediately to the east of the listed meadow at St. Johann im Pongau. As the tall grass on the meadow north of the football pitch apparently made a landing there unappealing, I opted for the meadow east of the railway line. Due to the prevailing valley wind, I approached from the south, which was only slightly disrupted by a construction crane on the other side of the river. Otherwise, the recently mown field seemed much more inviting. I flew just over the crane (on the right in the picture), but this didn’t interfere with the final approach. The windsock protruding from the tree is clearly visible even from a considerable height thanks to the colour contrast. I imagine that an approach from the north would also work, but you must definitely approach from the east of the high-voltage power line where it bends. In the southern part, the meadow is a bit bumpy, but otherwise – apart from some very slight, long, drawn-out undulations – it’s absolutely no problem. Best regards and many thanks for your efforts – knowing about landing options is invaluable! Andrea.
- 2 December 2021, Jan Lyczywek: Landing on 28 June 2020. Approach from south to north. The floodlight masts posed no problem at all. A ‘classic’ approach over the football pitch, passing between the two northern floodlight masts. Instead, we approached from the EAST of the floodlight masts, flying between the masts and the railway line over the grandstand building. A dead straight approach parallel to the railway line onto the easternmost part of the field, right next to the railway. In the old photos, this strip is still farmland; by 2020, it was all meadow. Flight:
- 21 July 2021, Matthias Knopf: I was recently in St. Johann/Pongau and had a look at the 100-schilling meadows. Apart from the high-voltage power line to the north (which is well known), unfortunately floodlight masts are also likely to be installed on the southern side. As the meadows are short, you have to start the flare manoeuvre early. This could lead to a collision with one of the floodlight masts (approx. 20 metres high). I believe these were erected later. I wouldn’t want to land here in a two-seater in crosswinds. I prefer the meadow to the right of that, next to the river; according to local residents, a female pilot (name unknown, presumably from Wiener Neustadt) is said to have landed there successfully. Please could you give your assessment and take this into account.

- 20 July 2021, Manfred Schwegler: Approach heading only 29 due to a telephone line to the west. Approach over tall trees. Watch out for the high-voltage power line to the south. During a site inspection on 16 July 2021, the southern meadow was partly planted with tall maize. The landing strip, approximately 250 metres long, was only about 40 metres wide at the start, narrowing to 25 metres. The landing threshold is ‘marked’ by a manure heap approx. 1 m high. After the harvest and in the spring, the field is likely to be easier to land on again. The northern meadow was overgrown with tall grass but was still suitable for landing; there were a few tyre tracks across it (which can be driven over, albeit with a bit of a bump). There is a single stake at the start of the meadow, so do not touch down too far in front of the tree standing alone on the embankment (approx. 30 metres before it is fine). There is a usable length of approx. 220 metres up to the telephone line. The telephone line can be crossed if necessary.

Feedback:
- 12 May 2023, Jan Lyczywek: Site visit on 12 May 2023. Landing direction is east to west only. Valley wind (on days with uniform thermals and no dominant regional wind) blows from east to west. Landings are therefore usually with a tailwind. The meadow is initially flat in the eastern part, but rises steeply in the western part. It is also somewhat undulating there. The power line on the southern side (on the left as viewed from the approach) of the meadow poses no problem. When approaching from the side, the meadow behind a rocky outcrop may be obscured or difficult to see. Site inspection video:
- 15 March 2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: North of the power line, the terrain slopes slightly to the left and also rises towards the end. To the left of the power line, the line itself is in the way of the final approach. This shortens the meadow considerably! The cross-wind approach is obscured by rocks.

- 15 March 2021, Roland Henz: Emergency landing area in the Montafon Föhn region; site visit on 7 September 2020. Notable features include the ski jumps to the south-west of the landing area and the tennis courts. In the approach area there are power lines and the surface of the meadows is undulating; the best section is likely to be in the meadow just before the houses, though it would probably be difficult to see due to tall vegetation. The adjacent meadow should also be suitable, but has a significantly shorter approach. The preferred landing direction is north-east; landing in the opposite direction might also be possible if necessary. Although it was clear on that day and therefore suitable for landing, the risk of it being blocked seems relatively high. The Audi team were also active, though only in the very northern part, which wouldn’t be a problem. However, a little further south-east (almost right next to it) there is something that looks like a former sports ground. When approaching from the north, you still have to fly over some flagpoles, but after that the field is relatively flat. Land in the rear section on the right-hand side (there’s a ditch on the left). My suggestion: if you consider both options (the former airfield and the former sports ground), this would be a Category B site.

Valley wind (on days with consistent thermals and no dominant regional wind) from the east. Slight cross-slope from north (road) to south (river); not a problem. Possibly slightly damp, as it is lower-lying and closer to the river. Reconnaissance video: south-western meadow:.

Surface: Grassland

Updated: 10 April 2013
Reported hazards: uneven ground, power lines/other cables
Feedback:
- 14 March 2009, Ulrich Plarre: Meadow (1): The meadow lies directly north-east of the valley station of the large ‘Col Rodella’ cable car, which runs north-north-west. A prominent feature is a high-voltage power line consisting mainly of concrete pylons. The landing is from south-west to north-east, with an approach along the valley axis, passing east of the valley station and east of the prominent high-voltage power line. Watch out for tall trees and the green safety netting (2 m high) whilst still on approach. Touch down late! From the valley station, the concrete-pylon power line runs in a straight line north-east towards Canazei. The landing area begins to the east of this line, next to an iron lattice mast, hugging the eastern slope! There is also a windsock here! Ahead of this, the first 100 metres of the meadow are undulating and a small transverse ridge hinders a smooth landing (now a golf driving range)! After that, the terrain improves, though it becomes increasingly narrow! To the right (west) of the power line, where the windsock is also located, the ground is more undulating, but it would still be possible to land right next to the high-voltage line! It is only further north – i.e. at the end of the field – that several 50 cm high iron posts, positioned across the direction of landing, appear at intervals of approx. 20 m!
- 14 March 2009, Ulrich Plarre: Meadow (2):. This meadow lies approximately 600 m north-east of Meadow (1) in the direction of Canazei. It, too, lies along the continuation of the concrete-pylon power line, though to the north-west of it. It is approximately 300 m long. The south-western threshold is formed by two barns 50 paces apart. Land to the west, near the footpath, as a embankment borders the meadow to the east. The cross-wind approach, which runs diagonally to the valley axis, should begin above the Col Rodella valley station and lead over the western row of trees (approx. 10 m high) that border the meadow area. The final section is unfortunately very short, but feasible.
- 14 March 2009, Ulrich Plarre: The part of the meadow used by paragliders is very undulating and not recommended.

The bump in the ground mentioned was only faintly visible due to the tall grass, but it was a nuisance and a hazard. Feedback:
- 15 December 2023, Benjamin Bachmaier: I visited the site in September 2023. I took a video of each location; here are the links, followed by a brief summary for all three meadows: Schluden’s former model aeroplane airfield: Unfortunately, the model aeroplane airfield has been closed, but the meadow still exists. Irrigation poles have been installed; the swath between them is approximately 23 metres wide and 250 metres long. Aerial irrigation is likely to be barely visible. This means the landing conditions are similarly good/poor to those at ‘Schluderns South’. Schluderns North: Runs parallel to the road, where the paragliding windsock is also located. A few metres to the south-east, running parallel to the road, is the first row of irrigation pipes; then there is 25 metres of space until the next row of irrigation pipes, followed by another 25 metres of space – in other words, two parallel ‘lanes’. Irrigation from the air is likely to be barely visible. Heading towards 04, a dirt track appears after 180 metres; however, it is level and should be easy to drive over at low speed. The actual end only comes after 280 metres in the form of a bump in the ground.
- 9 August 2023, Hans Trautenberg: Webcam showing the old airfield, as well as the section where festivals are occasionally held.
- 15 March 2021, Jakob Edmaier: Landing direction exclusively NW. The model catch net extends into the field and limits the width at its narrowest point to approx. 30 m. Flat valley floor. Contact model flying club:

Feedback:
- 19 October 2025, Jan Lyczywek: Site visit on 18 October 2025. A wide, flat valley floor with several potential landing sites in the wider vicinity. The marked meadow is suitable, but between the eastern edge of the meadow and the side road branching off the main road in an arc, there is a power line running north-south parallel to the eastern edge of the meadow! An approach from east to west is therefore NOT recommended, or at best would require a longer landing on the fields further to the west, which are, however, much shorter. An approach from west to east into the marked meadow, on the other hand, is perfectly feasible. The recommended landing direction is therefore the 10th. Site inspection video:

- 16 May 2023, Jan Lyczywek: Site visit on 12 May 2023. Meadow unchanged. Unobstructed approach from south to north. Large, flat meadow. Unfortunately, it is divided into small sections across the valley axis by paths, ditches and possibly also fences. The area marked on the map is presumably the longest strip running in the direction of the valley, but at just under 200 metres it is very short; hence it is only rated B. This is also where the paragliders’ landing area with a windsock is located. Site inspection video, part 1: Site inspection video, part 2:
- 6 March 2021, Bernd Korthaus: This is a landing area for paragliders. Please take care, as the map also shows a playground there. The approach is from the south via the Burgschrofen, a small wooded hill with a chapel. Not visible from above is a metal-lined stream to the south of the meadow. I can’t remember whether the path in front of the meadow is passable by glider, so I would land immediately behind the path. That leaves just under 200 metres of landing run before the road. The meadows south of the stream also look suitable for landing, but there were wooden posts there.

Reported hazards: Electricity and other cables
Feedback:
- 14 May 2023, Johannes Schwemmer: Visit by car on 12 May 2023 to the Unterwössen cross-country flying week. The Embach meadow is difficult to approach as you’re flying uphill past trees and a fence. If you have too little momentum, you’ll land roughly on the slope once you’ve cleared the fence. If you have too much momentum, you’ll fly over the crest, past the high-voltage pylons and the houses behind them. The Embach meadows are a stopgap solution anyway. Anyone who can’t make it to Zell am See – perhaps due to headwinds – should first aim to land on the lovely meadows in St. Johann im Pongau, 20 km away, as they’re flat. If that’s not enough either, you’re in a bit of a pickle. In our opinion, the lesser of two evils is then Embach East. After all, right next to this meadow (Embach) stands an impressive fire station – help shouldn’t be far away. Stefan and I weren’t entirely sure whether this was a C-class landing area or not a landing area at all. Hopefully, no one will have to decide that whilst flying a glider. Video here – please note, this isn’t Embach Ost but Embach; we initially got them mixed up when we were there.

Feedback:
- 15 March 2025, Marco Weiß: Everything’s still just as Jan described. Although I’d say landing over the barn onto the northernmost meadow is a bit of a challenge. You really do need to factor in an extra 20 metres of altitude that you’ll need to lose. I’d go for the second one. Today there was also a yellow windsock standing in the ditch in front of the hut on the eastern side of the road, about 50 metres away. Otherwise, brilliant!
- 8 November 2024, Jan Lyczywek: Site visit on 8 November 2024. Meadow unchanged. Approach and landing only from east to west. A total of 5 parallel landing strips, all over 300 metres long, level and without any gradient. All strips are separated from one another by clearly visible longitudinal ditches. The northernmost strip is the widest, measuring 22 metres at its narrowest point. Approach over a single barn. The second-northernmost strip is slightly narrower, 20 metres wide, but offers a completely unobstructed approach from the east. The three southernmost strips are significantly narrower. Nevertheless, they are still suitable for landing even with larger wingspans, as no posts are expected along the longitudinal ditches. All strips end in the west at a small hill that rises sharply. With a short roll-out, it should be possible to roll up the slope a little way. The longitudinal ditches end at the foot of the small hill. Valley wind (on days with consistent thermals and no penetrating supra-regional wind) is likely to come from the north, resulting in a crosswind. Category B solely due to the narrow width of the strips; otherwise it would be Category A. Site inspection video:
- 15 March 2021, Gerhard Hiemesch: SIP Category B. Long trenches!!! Obstacle-free wild meadow; landing only possible in direction 27, with a hill at the end. If approaching from the opposite direction, the approach is too high, making the meadow too short! The ground is rather soft, so a strong braking effect is to be expected.
- 11 March 2009, Christian (DD): initial feedback

- 1 June 2024, Jan Lyczywek: Inspection on 1 June 2024. The meadow remains in very good condition. A long, flat meadow between the road and a power line running parallel to it. Caution: roughly halfway across the meadow, a power line branches off at right angles and crosses the meadow from the triangular (A-shaped) electricity pylon to the bus shelter by the road. This cross-line is difficult to see. Nevertheless, in my opinion, the meadow is Category A, as both landing directions are possible. In both landing directions, one could either land on the first half of the meadow (as seen from the approach direction) and come to a halt before the cross-line, or roll past it, or fly over the cross-line and then land on the second half of the meadow (as seen from the approach direction). Site inspection video:

Amended: 08.07.2026
Reported hazards: uneven ground
Feedback:
- 8 July 2026, Zoltan Kovats: Field landing on 22 March 2026. The southernmost field, just south of the power line, was approached from the east over the houses, allowing for a crosswind approach with a short final. Short wooden posts mark out the plots and are only visible during the final approach; they are barely visible at all, particularly when the grass is tall.
- 5 July 2021, Roland Bieri: The marked meadows lie across the valley; an approach from the directions indicated is not possible! Between the first and second meadows (viewed from the south), a power line runs across the valley (Figure 2). This makes an approach in the direction of the valley difficult. The main problem, however, is that the fields running along the valley are too short, and a landing would have to be made across several fields with varying vegetation, ditches and terraces. The field slightly to the north (Figure 3) is better situated, but it also has a power line and posts that are difficult to see.

Surface: Grassland

Feedback:
- 7 March 2024, Patrick Gabler: There are currently no irrigation poles in the meadow. However, this may be the case depending on the season.
- 26 April 2022, Rudolf Schneider: Inspection on 23 April 2022: The irrigation system is currently in place. The steel poles are approximately 4 cm thick and 90 cm high. You should be able to see them during the final approach to land within the clear strips. Once the system has been dismantled, we have a first-class landing field 350 m long, level and completely smooth. See the satellite image below for the sprinkler layout. There is a wall running alongside the road, and the first row of sprinklers (red dots) runs parallel to it, approximately 2 m away. There are then clear strips, each approximately 31 m wide, leading up to the next row of sprinklers.
- 15 March 2021, Roland Henz: Actually a good meadow and one of the few heading south towards the Jauffen Pass. The irrigation systems were also a problem during the site visit on 24 September 2016. The distance would be suitable for at least 15 m and 18 m, but would probably not be visible if the vegetation were taller. The irrigation pipes are also visible on Street View!

en\u001fInfo: Power line in the east Obe:
Information: Power line in the east
Surface: Grass Land
Direction: 04/-
Tour: 2025
Amended: 20.04.2025
Reported hazards: Electricity and other cables
Feedback:
- 19 April 2025, Michael Buchthal: Site visit south of the Kinzig on 23 March, north of the Kinzig on 6 April 2025: Regardless of the length of the fields, the approach is very challenging in the Kinzig Valley, which is already quite narrow at this point. Picture 1: south of the Kinzig; Picture 2: north of the Kinzig.
- 24 March 2024, Marcus Neubronner: Here’s another link to a video of the landing field in Wolfach.
- 25 February 2024, Marcus Neubronner: Site visit on 25 February 2024. In my view, the meadow south of the main road mentioned is very difficult to use. On approach, there are various power lines to the east, as well as a tree in the middle of the meadow. This makes it difficult for aircraft with a large wingspan (18m+). It might be feasible for a wooden aeroplane or a club-class aircraft with some skill. When approaching from the west, you also have to fly over industrial buildings, so in either case you don’t really have a clear approach. The field on the northern side of the main road is also very challenging, as it is even shorter but significantly wider. If you touch down early, you’ll have about 200 metres to roll out. The approach from west to east is largely unobstructed, with the exception that you have to fly over the main road right at the very end. It’s essential to time your approach so that there isn’t a large coach parked in front of the meadow. All in all, as things stand today, this option seems better to me than the meadow currently listed. I’d feel confident attempting a landing in a Club Class aircraft. With an 18-metre aircraft, I’d only do so in a genuine emergency. So, exercise caution.
en\u001fInfo: Power line on the road cro:

Surface: Grass Land
Direction: 04/22
Tour: 2025
Amended: 20.04.2025
Reported hazards: Electricity and other cables
Feedback:
- 19 April 2025, Michael Buchthal: Inspection of the meadow in front of the wind turbines on 23 March, and the main field on 6 April 2025: The meadow in front of the wind turbines can be approached either directly from the east across the road (Figure 2), or across open terrain from the north-east (Figure 5) or south (Figure 3). The area measuring 250 x 220 m should be sufficiently large given the unobstructed approach; the north-western areas are also suitable for landing (Figure 5). As can be seen in the overview, the main field (Figures 6–9) is divided into two sections; crossing the boundary should be avoided where possible. The fact that this landing area is immediately bordered by the adjacent woods makes the approach more challenging for gliders with a larger wingspan. The path along the north-eastern threshold is frequently used.

Feedback:
- 2 May 2022, Rudolf Schneider: Smooth meadow with no sprinkler system. Running across slightly south of the centre, at a height of approx. 3–4 m, a thin wire (telephone?) is suspended from two flimsy wooden slats. See satellite image with sketch. Landing from the south, approaching over the trees, is fairly straightforward. Short landing! Touch down before the wire! Landing from the north, approaching over Quellenhof with a very steep descent, is quite difficult if you want to touch down before the wire. In an emergency, the wire is likely to give way.

Information: Take the meadow in Tösens when possible, Water ditches!
Surface: Field
Direction: 15/33
Tour: 2020
Amended: 07.03.2021
Reported hazards: uneven ground, electrical and other cables
Feedback:
- 21 February 2021, Andreas Hudler: North of Prutz, east of the Inn and the main road. Large area sloping gently northwards. Both landing directions possible, north preferred: terrain not a factor, keep to the right, close to the path. Landing direction south: in mountain winds, approach towards the pylons of the cross-country power line. Fly low to the left past the pylon and touch down behind it. Quality: very good. Note: Watch out for vegetation and choose the best part of the meadow.
- 27 March 2011, Ulrich Plarre: initial feedback
- 24 May 2005, Peter Stackler: Last weekend I had the pleasure of flying cross-country in Prutz/Oberinntal with an ASH 25 fitted with winglets. I also opted for the field listed in the SIP, although I chose the one running parallel to the main road after the village of Faggen (which is an extension of the field you recommended), approaching from south to north, as your field might have been too short. During the crosswind approach, watch out for high-voltage pylons; it’s best to begin the final approach over the houses in Faggen and keep well to the left (close to the main road) as there are hay barns on the right – should the landing run out of distance, it’s possible to glide under the power lines (there’s sufficient lateral clearance), though this wasn’t the case for me. The condition of the field is ideal, with no bumps or ditches; there’s a tarmac road right next to it, and an Esso petrol station for refreshments whilst you wait is about 8 minutes away.
- 25 March 2005, Thomas Bauer: To the north of the airfield, there has been a large petrol station with a lorry workshop and lorry yard by the road for at least three years. For this reason, the meadow shown in SIP has become smaller. However, it is still sufficient. The issue of the ditches must be taken seriously. These are not irrigation ditches that you might be able to drive over – they are concrete channels overgrown with grass.!!!

Feedback:
- 21 March 2025, Andreas Weitz: Site visit March 2025. The southern, shorter meadow is very flat compared to the others. There is a fence along the eastern path; two individual posts stand in the farmyard to the south of the meadow; and there are some individual posts along the western edge. An approach to the more southerly meadow is possible from both directions. When landing towards the west, you must fly over the fence by the path; when landing towards the east, you should also avoid flying too low due to the occasional single posts. Take care also because of the trees. The farmer seems to mow his meadows in different patterns from time to time, which can sometimes split the meadows in two (see satellite images from recent years). Further south, directly by the southern path – where landings have already taken place – there are significantly more bumps, some of which are very high.
- 21 March 2025, Andreas Weitz: Site visit March 2025. The southern, shorter meadow is very flat compared to the others. There is a fence along the eastern path; at the farmyard south of the meadow there are two individual posts; and along the western edge there are some individual posts. The farmer seems to mow his meadows in different ways from time to time, which means the meadows can sometimes be divided (see satellite images from recent years). Further south, directly by the southern path – where landings have already taken place – there are significantly more bumps in the ground, some of which are very high.
- 6 June 2023, Benjamin Bachmaier: Landed in May 2023, no change.
- 17 June 2021, Hans Trautenberg: The meadows to the north, closer to the road, also look very good from the air; there’s even a windsock at the mountain rescue hut there. But these meadows have bumps in the ground as high as a man.

- 30 June 2026, Markus Full: The fields along the road are only moderately suitable. There are lots of posts and a mobile chicken coop that keeps being moved around. The fields to the west are much more suitable.
- 15 July 2021, Markus Full: Some of the meadows are blocked by mobile chicken coops.
- 15 June 2011, Ulrich Plarre: initial feedback
- 15 June 2011, Sven Richter: On 16 April, I made an off-field landing in Farchant-Nord myself. The meadow is flat and wide enough even for a DG1000. Due to the valley wind, a northbound landing is advisable. Contrary to previous comments, I would not overrun any of the cross-runways. The embankments are steep enough to send an aeroplane back into the air even at around 60 km/h. If you get too far, it’s better to deploy the air brakes and fly over the second cross-path. After getting out, I noticed the iron bars used to cordon off the western part of the field. They cannot be seen from the air. If you’re aware of this and aim for the eastern part along the road, it’s no problem. The remaining 35 metres in width are sufficient. A lovely field – I can highly recommend it. It looks even better in real life than the photos suggest.

There are 22-metre-wide gaps between the trees at the thresholds! Difficult approach! In front of the northern threshold, the terrain drops vertically for 100 metres! Two wind socks are in place!

Landing feels like landing in a saucepan! Better to use Sterzing or Obervintl!
- 3 November 2008, Ulrich Plarre: Another good 400-metre-long meadow is 3 km to the south, west of the Eisack River, near Neustift Abbey! No power lines; approach only from south to north, slightly ascending!

Surface: Field

Inspected: 2024
Updated: 1 May 2024
Comments:
- 1 May 2024, Benjamin Bachmaier: I visited the site in September 2023. Given the tall grass and undulating ground, I couldn’t definitively identify the entry ‘Schluderns 2022’ as a possible landing site, but I couldn’t rule it out beyond doubt either. I’m not sure what to do about it – perhaps leave it as a ‘C’? According to the description, it’s difficult to identify the correct strip, and at 180m (?) it’s really extremely short. However, I was able to confirm that the three areas marked in the uploaded image are each suitable for landing, at least with a 15–18-metre aircraft. I would list all three as Category B. I took a video of each one; here are the links, followed by a brief summary for all three meadows: Schluderns South: I can confirm the previous entries. Approximately 23 metres wide, 250 metres long, with a relatively unobstructed approach from both sides. The width of the irrigation channel appears to be fixed, and the path between them has looked unchanged for years. The irrigation is probably barely visible from the air.
- 5 September 2021, Benjamin Bachmaier: Following the closure of the Schluderns model aeroplane airfield, we have found another (emergency) landing site further south. Unlike almost all other fields throughout the Vintschgau, this one is not criss-crossed with irrigation poles or used for fruit cultivation, but is an open field with just enough length, though only wide enough for small aircraft. Irrigation poles on the left and right edges of the “runway”, spaced approx. 22–24 metres apart. Clear in between. Length just under 250 metres. Unobstructed approach from the south-east; trees in the north-west approx. 100 metres before the threshold, with maize in front of them. Site visit on 16 August 2021; information and images: Markus Full. Google Street View from 2011 is available. At that time, it was still arable land, with no grass.

- 5 May 2021, Klaus-Dieter Degen: Last inspection 4 May 2021. No changes from the previous description; use only in an emergency!
- 10 November 2003, Mathias Schunk: This field should only be considered as an absolute emergency landing site. It is situated just 2.5 km from the Spitzingsattel, which is at the same altitude. With a reasonably sensible approach plan for this field, it is therefore also possible to fly safely over the Spitzingsattel, which means that Fischhausen (1.22.), just 4 km away and situated at 800 m, is available as a normal alternative landing site.
- 10 November 2003, Hans Fitterer: Certainly not a ‘normal’ off-airfield landing site, but perfectly suitable for a safe landing; however, it is relatively damp and cannot be accessed by car; this means the aircraft should be dismantled and carried to the trailer. It is essential to inspect the site on foot and from the aircraft. When approaching from the south at a low altitude, the field remains out of sight until the very end. – Landing from the south is possible, though visibility is poor due to the forest; landing from the north may also be possible.

- 15 May 2023, Johannes Schwemmer: Visit by car on 12 May 2023 to the Unterwössen Cross-Country Flying Week. Meadow Plan A (blue) is free of obstacles as long as you approach along the main road. Surface: Horse Race Track
Direction: 04/-
Visited: 2009
Updated: 27 March 2009
Reported hazards: uneven ground, obstacles on the ground
Feedback:
- 15 March 2021, Roland Henz: A highly questionable emergency landing site which must under no circumstances be relied upon, yet is still listed in various landing guides – presumably also because the Merano Model Sports Team occasionally uses this field: And there has in fact already been at least one successful emergency landing by a powered aircraft on 12 May 2008 (see the BFU Bulletin 2008 under reference number SX002-0/08 and a newspaper report). Nevertheless, anyone who has the opportunity should go and assess the conditions for themselves on site. Apart from the fact that people and animals may well be present here during events – and indeed at other times too – it is uncertain whether a new obstacle course might not make landing impossible. Furthermore, any landing would have to be a high-precision manoeuvre with a high risk of damage; a wingspan of more than 15 metres would probably not be feasible! The field has therefore been classified as unsuitable for landing! Generally speaking: the area west of the Jauffen Pass, stretching into the Vintschgau valley as far as Schluderns, offers no landing options; this region must therefore be flown over with due caution! Otherwise, the Bolzano airfield to the south should always be planned as the landing site, whilst also taking into account the strong valley wind system from the south!
- 27 March 2009, CVAO: initial feedback
- 25 March 2005, Thomas Bauer: Completely flat. Large enough. Land on the southern side of the meadow, heading east or west if possible. The road by the meadow is called Flugplatzstr, I think. Perhaps you could get away from here via a microlight tow?
- 9 February 2004, Sepp Holzapfel: I was in Merano last year (by car) and took a close look at the trotting track there. It was cordoned off, so I could only inspect it from the outside, but what I saw there was absolutely not impressive. In my opinion, the track is so narrow – and, above all, bordered by a high hedge – that, with a lot of luck, a 13-metre ‘Spatz’ might just manage to land there. I consider it dangerous to recommend this track for 15-metre gliders that aren’t high-wing designs! Given the importance of this area, I believe it’s imperative to warn against entering this option into the computer as a possibility without first inspecting it in person.

Feedback:
- 2 April 2023, Urs Wolfensberger: The satellite image only shows the (preferred) route with an approach across the valley. Whilst the description is generally correct, I would suggest specifying an approach towards 31 (so as not to contradict Figures 5 and 6). When approaching from direction 31, the meadow does indeed lie to the left, but only then is the stated field length of 250m realistic; from direction 36, it is at most 170m. The considerable gradient of approx. 6% should also be noted. The entry dated 7 March 2021 is out of date. These residential buildings are workers’ accommodation from the former Gotthard Tunnel construction site, which was dismantled long ago. These are no longer visible on the satellite image used by streckenflug.at, nor in Ernst Will’s approach video dated 5 April 2020. I would delete Image 1 (as it also contradicts Image 4) and replace Images 5 and 6 with images that do not show the demolished houses (see also the appendix). For images 1 to 4, it would be helpful to include a note indicating the direction in which the photograph was taken – towards 09 or 27 (i.e. perpendicular to the 31 approach).
- 7 March 2021, Christian Hynek: Several residential buildings have been constructed in the eastern part of the meadow!

- 25 April 2023, Marco Maceri: Dear Streckenflug.at, last Wednesday I landed on the meadow in Dubino. The landing direction of the meadow is approximately 33, i.e. 90° to the one currently shown on the website. The approach began at around 200 m, using the church tower in Delebio as a reference point, approximately 2 km south-east of the meadow. The landing spot was actually a narrow (about 25 m) strip of dark brown, fertilised ground in the middle of the meadow, clearly visible from Delebio. The meadow itself conceals hazards, such as slightly rough ground beneath the grass and a few wooden stakes driven into the ground. From above, I did not notice the meadow shown on the website or the landing direction 04/22 as being possible.
- 10 April 2013, Christian (DD): initial feedback

Surface: Grass Land

Amended: 29.06.2026
Reported hazards: Electricity and other cables
Feedback:
- 28 May 2026, Konrad Mahlknecht: Visited today; everything remains the same.
- 16 May 2023, Jan Lyczywek: Site visit on 12 May 2023. Meadow unchanged. Large, flat area, completely unobstructed approach; power lines pose no problem whatsoever. Unfortunately, the fields are divided into small plots running perpendicular to the valley axis; some are used for maize cultivation. The best meadow is probably in the north-eastern corner of the marked area; there is also a paragliding landing site there with a windsock. Site visit video, part 1: Site visit video, part 2:
- 15 March 2021, Alexander Keupp: This is the launch site from the 2011 SIP. I visited the site on the ground in the summer of 2019. It is suitable for landing, but the ideal landing area is quite short (approx. 200 m). Be sure to watch out for the post and the bumps in the ground (see diagram). The field shown in the diagram is also used by the local paragliding school.
- 15 March 2021, Jan Lyczywek: Please note: no site inspection has been carried out to date (as at 3 May 2020); there may be a power line in the southern quarter, so only Category B for the time being! Valley wind (on thermal days with no wider regional wind) from the north.
en\u001fLandefeld ok. Ab ca. August ist: Landing area is OK. From around August onwards, the southern part – that is, below the cross-country path – is blocked by grazing cattle, but the northern part, up to the road, is still suitable for landing 15- and 18-metre aircraft, as it slopes upwards.
en\u001fLandewiese Götzis streichen. Zäu: Cross out the Götzis landing meadow. Fences, livestock and the obtrusive Sattelberg. Better meadows to the south.
en\u001fLandewiese auf Plateau; 36 Landb: Landing field on a plateau; 36 can be landed with a left-hand approach across the valley. Slight undulations in the meadow; the slope rises. A cross-wind track at the end of the meadow may be passable. When approaching the 18, a high-voltage power line obstructs the approach and the terrain is sloping! The picture shows the view towards the south.
en\u001fLandung (ausnahmsweise nicht ich: Landing (for once, not by myself but by another pilot) on 9 May 2023. The meadow remains in good condition. More like grade A than B. The meadow rises slightly from east to west towards the pass summit. The meadow slopes from the south (road) towards the north (railway line). A few wooden pegs.
en\u001fLandung am 03.06.2019. Unverände: Landing on 3 June 2019. Still very good – perfectly flat, with no gradient; in my opinion, better than Erpfendorf. Valley wind (on days with consistent thermals and no strong, wide-ranging winds) from the north.
en\u001fLandung am 03.10.2021. Sehr eben: Landing on 3 October 2021. A very flat area with plenty of excellent meadows, some of which even run parallel to the valley. Valley wind (on days with consistent thermals and no strong, wide-ranging winds) from the east. Föhn channelled parallel to the valley from the west. Flight: ((The only question is whether one should even include individual meadows in the Inn Valley, where landing is generally easy.))
en\u001fLandung am 05.07.2024. Wiese wei: Landing on 5 July 2024. The field remained in very good condition. Good landing conditions. No damage to the field caused during landing. Removing the aircraft proved extremely difficult, as the farmer had blocked all access routes. No willingness whatsoever to find a mutual solution. The police, who were called, did not consider the matter to fall within their remit. [ WeGlide flight ]
en\u001fLandung am 07.06.2023 (ausnahmsw: A landing on 7 June 2023 (for once, not by me but by a fellow flight instructor) shows another good alternative near the catalogue meadows. [ WeGlide flight ]
en\u001fLandung am 10.09.2023. Landerich: Landing on 10 September 2023. Preferred landing direction: south-east to north-west (landing direction 32). A small, barely visible power line at the south-eastern end of the meadow, running along the access road at right angles to the main road. The south-eastern part of the meadow, between the main road on the left and a solitary house on the right, is very narrow (40 m wide) but level. The north-western part is wider, but has uneven ground and rises slightly towards the north-west at the end. The Gumping meadow (approx. 1 km further north) is preferred! [ WeGlide flight ]
en\u001fLandung am 12.06.2021. Wiese unv: Landed on 12 June 2021. The meadow remains in very good condition. Flight:
en\u001fLandung am 12.08.2021. Wiese ans: Landing on 12 August 2021. Meadow slopes from east to west. Landing is therefore only possible from east to west (landing direction 27). Meadow is partly undulating. Cross-slope from south to north. Valley inflow (on days with consistent thermals and no significant cross-regional wind) in the Achensee valley from the north. In the Pertisau side valley, the valley inflow presumably flows round the foot of the Seebergspitze and then continues up the side valley from the east (?). The valley looks narrow, but there is room for a standard traffic pattern: it makes sense to make the final approach on the southern edge of the village = the northern slope of the Zwölferkopf – this is the last chance for a slope wind; watch out for cable cars. Crosswind approach heading north, parallel to the shore above the lake. Final approach heading west over the houses on the northern edge of the village. Alternatives: the meadow further west (west of the westernmost houses) looks larger from the air, but is divided into very small sections. The meadows on the southern edge of the village – the northern slope of the Zwölferkopf – are also divided into sections. Flight:
en\u001fLandung am 13.08.2021, Anflugric: Landed on 13 August 2021, approached from east to west as recommended, landed slightly uphill; a really excellent meadow. Usable length more like 350–400 metres (measured using Google Maps). Flight:
en\u001fLandung am 15.05.2022. Sehr gute: Landing on 15 May 2022. Very good meadow, no slope, nice and flat. Deserves an A rather than a B. Approach from south to north between a farm and a row of trees. Mobile pasture fences are possible. [ WeGlide flight ]
en\u001fLandung am 18.08.2023. Talwind w: Landing on 18 August 2023. The valley wind was blowing down the valley (!) that day, i.e. from the south-west to the north-east. Apparently, that’s normal there. This is very handy for landing, as it means you can fly into the wind but still head up the valley (!) and have an unobstructed approach to the meadow, just as Holger has already described. [ WeGlide flight ]
en\u001fLandung am 18.08.2023. Wiese wie: Landing on 18 August 2023. The meadow is back to its original, excellent condition. The spoil/excavated material described by Holger in 2022 has been cleared away as planned, and the meadow has regained its full length between the ditch to the east and the sports pitch to the west.
en\u001fLandung am 20.06.2023 (ausnahmsw: A landing on 20 June 2023 (for once, not by me but by a fellow flight instructor) shows another, less favourable alternative near the Katalogwiesen. [ WeGlide flight ]
en\u001fLandung am 22.03.2023 (ausnahmsw: A landing on 22 March 2023 (for once, not by me but by a fellow flight instructor) shows another good alternative near the catalogue meadows. [ WeGlide flight ]
en\u001fLandung am 23.07.2023. Unverände: Landing on 23 July 2023. The outfield remains in good condition – no worse and no better than the numerous other fields in this area, which is very suitable for landing. Flying takes place here using a winch once or twice a year; outside these times, the field is not recognisable as an airfield. Please only use it if it has been freshly mown (if not, other fields in the surrounding area are actually better). Flight on WeGlide:
en\u001fLandung am 24.06.2023 (ausnahmsw: Landing on 24 June 2023 (for once, not by me, but by a fellow flight instructor). The meadow was still in very good condition. [ WeGlide flight ]
en\u001fLandung am 26.05.2022. Ebene Wie: Landing on 26 May 2022. Flat meadow, unobstructed approach from the north with a light tailwind. An approach from the south over the houses should also be possible, but the meadow is a little short in that case. Valley wind (on days with consistent thermals and no dominant regional wind) from the north. Several other good meadows and fields around the village of Aschau. Link to the flight:
en\u001fLandung am 28.06.2020. Anflug vo: Landing on 28 June 2020. Approach from south to north. The floodlight masts posed no problem at all. A ‘classic’ approach over the football pitch, passing between the two northern floodlight masts. Instead, we approached from the EAST of the floodlight masts, flying between the masts and the railway line over the grandstand building. A dead straight approach parallel to the railway line onto the easternmost part of the field, right next to the railway. In the old photos, this strip is still farmland; by 2020, it was all meadow. Flight:
en\u001fLandung direkt neben der Bundess: You can land right next to the main road, and if that doesn’t work out, you can also go down the small side street (behind the lamppost).
en\u001fLaut heutiger Sicht ist der Acke: From today’s vantage point, the field has disappeared again. I would once again rate the off-field landing conditions as ‘GOOD’. The turf was also relatively firm. Only in the area of the field from 2022 are there still a few molehills left. The turf isn’t as stable here.
en\u001fLee in approach, you can roll un: Lee in approach, you can roll under the high-voltage-cables
en\u001fLeicht wellig, aber nicht kritis: Slightly undulating, but not a major issue. A westbound approach is advisable, if possible. There is a tree in the southern meadow on the approach path. When approaching from the east, there is a major high-voltage power line, which makes a clean approach and a short landing difficult.
en\u001fLeitung am Westende. Rechts well: Track at the western end. Undulating on the right. [ WeGlide flight ]
en\u001fLeitung, Anflug durch Schneise z: Power line, approaching through a gap between trees
en\u001fLeitung. Anflug nur von Ortschaf: Route. It is recommended to approach only from the village towards the forest
en\u001fLine at the threshold poorly vis: Line at the threshold poorly visible, poles are between the trees!
en\u001fLine on approach 30, direction 1: Line on approach 30, direction 12 slightly uphill
en\u001fLine south of the meadow, meadow: Line south of the meadow, meadow hangs slightly
en\u001fMeadow on the north side is best: Meadow on the north side is best choice.
en\u001fMeadow on the south side of the: Meadow on the south side of the street really bumpy!
en\u001fMehrere landbare Wiesen im Talke: Several suitable landing meadows in the Saalfelden valley basin. The valley floor is not entirely flat. Valley winds (on thermal days with no wider-area wind) blow from the north. On Föhn days, an easterly wind is occasionally channelled parallel to the southern edge of the Steinernes Meer!
en\u001fMehrere parallele Streifen in No: Several parallel strips running north-south. Field boundaries clearly visible due to differences in colour. Fences and ditches in places along the field boundaries. Landing direction is south to north only. Valley wind (on thermal days with no wider-area wind) from the north-east. The landing strip shown here allows for a completely unobstructed approach from the south over a high embankment (aircraft carrier-style landing). The houses at the short end of the strip lie well below the level of the meadows and therefore do not constitute an obstacle.
en\u001fMiddle part of the long field pa: Middle part of the long field partly tilled!
I can confirm model aircraft flying. Not ultralights. The fields appear to be used solely for agricultural purposes and seem to be divided up somewhat depending on what is being grown. There was also a feeding trough right in the middle, with arable land to the right and left of it. Picking up trailers is difficult on the extremely narrow roads, especially with longer ones, so you may well have to push them.
en\u001fModellflugbetrieb und Ultraleich: Model aircraft and microlight operations. When approaching from the north or south during operations: if in doubt, fly a little higher, touch down before the dirt track and taxi past it.
en\u001fModellflugplatz kurz und schmal,: A small, narrow model aeroplane field, but with a perfectly maintained lawn and situated in the direction of the valley (wind direction). On days with thermals and no regional wind, the wind blows into the valley from the south. However, a regional northerly wind (northerly foehn) can reach right down to the ground!
en\u001fMultiple good landable fields in: Multiple good landable fields in this area.
en\u001fN Reco a pied indispensable. Att: A visual approach is essential. Land facing north.
en\u001fN/S Champ difficile. Fosses buse: N/S Difficult field. Drainage ditches in the middle of the field. Approach over the first field and land on the second. A walk-through reconnaissance is essential.
en\u001fN/S Reco a pied indispensable. P: N/S: On-foot reconnaissance essential. Mobile fencing may be required. Meadows.
en\u001fN/S Remonte vers le N. Attention: N/S Climbing towards the north. Take care in northerly winds (turbulence). Other fields may be used.
en\u001fN/S Remonte vers le N. Turbulenc: N/S Moving northwards. Turbulence from the north-west. Other fields may be possible.
en\u001fN/S Tres delicat. Reco a pied in: N/S Very tricky. A guide is essential. Lots of potholes.
en\u001fNach wie vor: Achensee Nord Nörd: As before: Achensee Nord – Nördliche Wiese has hidden pegs in the meadow. It’ll cost money to repair!
en\u001fNarrow valley, no normal approac: Narrow valley, no normal approach possible!
Narrow valley, no normal approach possible! First 50m unlandable!
Narrow valley, no normal approach possible! In the middle of the narrow there is a little swamp with 10m in diameter!
en\u001fNarrow valley, not much space fo: Narrow valley, not much space for last turn!
en\u001fNicht den kurzen Modellflugplatz: Don’t use the short model aeroplane airfield itself, but rather the long strips to the west (as shown here) or to the east. Valley wind (on thermal days with no wider-area wind) from the north.
en\u001fNorth wind blows most of the tim: North wind blows most of the time from the Lake, meadow rises to the side!
en\u001fNotlandefeld im Föhnareal Montaf: Emergency landing area in the Montafon Föhn region; site visit on 7 September 2020. Notable features include the ski jumps to the south-west of the landing area and the tennis courts. In the approach area there are power lines and the surface of the meadows is undulating; the best section is likely to be in the meadow just before the houses, though it would probably be difficult to see due to tall vegetation. The adjacent meadow should also be suitable, but has a significantly shorter approach. The preferred landing direction is north-east; landing in the opposite direction might also be possible if necessary. As this is one of the most important Föhn areas, every potential landing option here is crucial!
en\u001fNur Landerichtung 13 möglich (in: Only landing direction 13 is possible (into the valley ahead). A difficult final approach over two successive high-voltage power lines (height approx. 70 m), as well as a group of houses. Approx. 10–15 m after the start of the field, there is still a difference in height within the field of approx. 1.5 m. After the stated 160 m, a path crosses the field.
en\u001fNördlich am Waldrand Stromleitun: To the north, at the edge of the woodland, there is a power line, but this does not pose an obstacle during the approach; the field is still 150 metres wide to the south of the power line. The approach from the west is almost the same as for the airfield; the field begins with a barbed-wire fence. The meadow rises slightly towards the north-east; there are no ditches, etc. The width of the valley allows for normal cross-wind approaches. In my view, it is also possible to land from the east, although in that case you have to fly over 5 metres of scrub; the meadow also slopes slightly downwards in that direction.

Feedback:
- 16 February 2021, Robert Zinnecker: The runway is fully operational and in very good condition. Grass is encroaching on the taxiway, but it is still easy for powered aircraft to taxi on. It is best to taxi off the runway only at the far south end, in the middle (near the hangars) and at the far north end. No airfield manager. Only submit blind reports. The fuel station is not in operation. Infrastructure is in place, but in poor condition (e.g. toilets). There is a club with a few powered aircraft, as well as the region’s rescue helicopter, based at the airfield.

Surface: Bog/Swamp

Reported hazards: Power lines/other cables
Feedback:
- 20 May 2025, Rene Fischer: Here are two additional photos. The field is significantly longer than the stated 250 m. Paragliders land on the section marked in red.
- 7 August 2024, Rene Fischer: Hello. I was there last year and saw both the old and the new sites. The old one is indeed a disaster, but the new one (featured in your catalogue) is now a landing site for paragliders, complete with a windsock and two large open areas. I walked around it and took a few photos as well. I’d rate it at least a B, if not an A, although I’m not familiar with your standards. It’s bound to be a bit bumpy, of course. But I wouldn’t have any qualms about landing there. I’ve got the photos on my mobile and can send them if needed. We’re also welcome to have a chat on the phone. Regards, Rene. 00393917481600

en\u001fOberfläche: Field Besichtigung::
Surface: Field
Tour: 2004
Amended: 07.03.2004
Feedback:
- 15 October 2003, Werner Amann: I’ve had a look at the site in Zug and would describe it as an EMERGENCY LANDING AREA.
- 15 October 2003, Christian Holzner: I really cannot recommend Zug. Between the 2,500-metre-high Omeshorn on the south side and the 2,200-metre-high Palmenalpe on the north side, the Zug Valley (1,500 m) is extremely narrow. A landing approach becomes a problem! In these high-alpine regions, it’s not just a question of looking at the meadow, but above all of how you actually get onto that meadow in the first place. What use is a meadow perhaps 150 metres long if you need a helicopter just to make the approach? For me, at any rate, approach options are just as important as the nature of the landing site; a good approach is a prerequisite for a good landing – this applies to the traffic pattern at the airfield, but even more so to a field landing.

Location: Field
Survey: 2008
Last updated: 18 July 2021
Reported hazards: Power lines/other cables
Feedback:
- 18 July 2021, Christian Hynek: WGC Catalogue 2008

Surface: Field
Tour: 2023
Amended: 07.01.2024
Reported hazards: uneven ground, electrical and other cables
Feedback:
- 6 January 2024, Jürgen Wisbacher: I would like to confirm Albert’s comment and correct Roland’s. Roland writes: The airfield is “only approachable from the south”, but he probably meant “only approachable from north to south”. If you look at his example flight, you can see that he chose the approach from the north. When approaching from the south, the high-voltage power line is in the way.
- 28 May 2021, Albert Kiessling: I cannot understand the field length of 700 metres, nor the statement that they are ‘only accessible from the south’. The fields running east-west all cross the direction of the furrows and extend beyond field boundaries. I doubt that these are A-fields. The fields marked in yellow (260 metres long) are fine and are situated right next to the model aeroplane airfield. I dare to doubt that the model airfield itself (46:39:41N 13:01:22E), with a length of 120 metres and a width of 6 metres, is suitable for landing.
- 15 March 2021, Roland Henz: Alternative to the east: Unproblematic farmland, although there are a number of power lines throughout the area; consequently, due to a major power line, this field can only be approached from the south!
- 27 March 2009, Rudi Frenslich: initial feedback

Surface: Field
Tour: 2026
Amended: 03.07.2026
Feedback:
- 11 May 2026, Simon Lemmerer: The situation remains the same; a visit on a day with a light southerly foehn, with a strong headwind on landing.
- 11 April 2022, Franz Tösch: Meadow – OK
- 17 July 2021, Simon Lemmerer: The field near Eisenerz is definitely suitable for landing smaller-span gliders in an emergency. The field right next to Eisenerz is no longer suitable for landing. The problem here is the valley wind, which usually comes from the west. However, you can roll out about 40 metres further south of the car wash.

- 18 July 2021, Manfred Schwegler: Only if you cannot reach Flp Nötsch! 15 July 2021: The meadow was in good condition, freshly mown, during the site inspection. A solid steel pipe stands in the meadow approximately 220 metres from the western edge and approximately 90 metres from the southern edge, overgrown with tall grass. There are also a few smaller wooden posts scattered about. An approx. 50m-wide strip in the southern section, running parallel to the motorway, would probably be the safest landing area at present to avoid the post. With an easterly wind and aircraft with good flap performance, the approach could possibly also be made from the west over the tall trees, given the length of the meadow (approx. 500 metres).

- 19 March 2023, Jan Lyczywek: photos from the video
- 19 March 2023, Jan Lyczywek: Site visit on 14 March 2023. The fields near Montmaur are listed as Field No. 311 on page 136 in the official outfield landing catalogue ‘Guide des aires de sécurité dans les Alpes, Édition 3’. Two areas are marked there; the field described here, “Montmaur Nord”, is the northern and slightly poorer of these two areas. It slopes upwards from south to north, so landing from south to north is preferable. Take care when flying low over the river and road from the south: a low-hanging power line with concealed pylons crosses the southern, pointed end of the meadow in a roughly west-east direction. Site inspection videos: Part 1: Part 2:

- 9 June 2025, Jan Lyczywek: Landed on 9 June 2025. Fields still in very good condition. [ WeGlide flight ]
- 14 March 2023, Jan Lyczywek: Inspection on 14 March 2023. Excellent fields. Slightly ascending from south to north. Landing in calm conditions therefore from south to north. When there is a southerly wind, it is also possible to land from north to south. The very longest field lies directly to the east, parallel to the road, but the longest of the fields to the west of the road is also very good. Do not drive over the field boundaries (=colour boundaries). Irrigation systems are laid underground, but the individual sprinklers do not appear to be permanently in place; in any case, there were none there in mid-March. Site visit video:

Surface: Field
Direction: 04/22
Tour: 2025
Amended: 20.04.2025
Feedback:
- 19 April 2025, Michael Buchthal: Site visit on 6 April 2025: The marked landing area must have been selected on the basis of satellite images without an on-site inspection. With a cross-slope of approx. 45°, it has never been suitable for gliders to land on (Figures 2 and 3). The landing site from my 1996 catalogue of out-of-field landing sites was located down by the road (marked in red, Figures 4), and, due to the taller trees on the south-western side and a power line in the north-east, is now only suitable as an emergency landing site. The only option I can see here are the two meadows at the top of the ridge; however, at 200 metres and with an approach across the valley, they too can only serve as a stopgap solution (Figures 5 and 6).

- 9 September 2023, Kilian Raemy: I am 58 years old and have lived in Plaffeien all my life; I have been paragliding since 1986/87, flying a delta in 1988 and a two-seater delta in 1990. I also hold a fixed-wing pilot’s licence and this year I passed my solo flight proficiency test on a glider. From the perspective of an ASK 21 pilot, I consider this field to be a good option. It can usually be used along its entire length, as I have marked it. Even though they are separate fields, they combine to form a huge, long area. And I wouldn’t describe the meadow as slightly sloping, but rather as flat. Of course, I’ll leave it up to you whether you’d prefer to classify the field as Category B. I’ve just provided a few recent aerial photos, taken on 2 September 2023. I hope this is of use to you. Kind regards, Kili
- 6 January 2022, Bernhard Bärfuss: Due to the buildings in Plaffeien, it’s better to approach from direction 05. Otherwise, there are few suitable outlanding fields in this area.

Feedback:
- 2 July 2024, Jan Lyczywek: Site visit on 25 June 2024. Approach possible from both directions. Minimal cross-slope from the road in the north down to the river in the south. The power line running alongside the road does not cause any obstruction. The field is quite stony; apart from that, it is Category A. Previously, a transverse field boundary was visible on Google Maps, dividing the area at its narrowest point into two roughly equal sections. This field boundary was definitely no longer present during the on-site inspection on 25 June 2024; the field has clearly been tilled and sown in a single continuous stretch along its full length. Should this remain the case in the long term, a very important new landing site has been created, making the route to Mont Ventoux significantly safer! Site inspection video:

Surface: Field
Direction: 09/27
Inspection: 2025
Amended: 5 June 2025
Feedback:
- 4 June 2025, Jan Lyczywek: Inspected on 2 June 2025. Unchanged factors supporting a Category A classification: - Unchanged shape of the field. - More than adequate length. - Very level ground. - No gradient whatsoever. - Sufficient unobstructed approach from east to west. - Approach is therefore against the valley breeze blowing from the west. A new, but unfortunately only temporary, factor in favour of Category A: - at the moment (early June 2025), the field is covered in recently harvested grain! Great! A factor in favour of Category B: - It is unclear what will be grown there next. Will it be ploughed up? Will maize be planted again? Site inspection video:
- 27 June 2023, Thomas Reinhold: Currently (27 June 2023), there is only very low vegetation in the western part between the path and the row of trees, offering a good opportunity. However, this could change quickly!
- 25 March 2023, Jan Lyczywek: Site visit on 24 March 2023. Field unchanged. In spring, as long as the maize hasn’t grown tall yet and the irrigation pipes haven’t been laid, this is a very good, Grade A field. Later in the year, however, it will be inaccessible due to the maize and irrigation pipes – see Christian Heiting’s note! New site visit video:
- 30 March 2022, Jan Lyczywek: Site visit by friends on 29 March 2022. A good field now in March whilst the vegetation is low. However, the irrigation pipes were already in place. High vegetation possible in summer! Site inspection video: The field near Espinasses is listed as Field No. 317 on pages 142–143 in the French out-of-bounds catalogue “Guide des aires de sécurité dans les Alpes, Édition 3”.

- 15 March 2021, Jan Lyczywek: Partially used for maize cultivation? Total length 500 m, divided by two farm tracks/wheel tracks (possibly passable?). In the eastern third, a power line crosses diagonally; this can be flown under when landing from the west. Valley wind (on thermal days with no wider-area wind) from the east. When a strong southerly foehn is blowing, the wind is from the west!

Surface: Field

Amended: 11.03.2009
Feedback:
- 11 October 2003, Florian Forcher: It’s a shame – yesterday I was sad to discover that the lovely meadow had been ploughed up and is now being used to grow winter barley. The field to the left of this one has also recently been ploughed up. In the photo, you can see a slight dip on the left (= west); there are also two landing options there, each on a strip of meadow approximately 20–30 metres wide. To the west of the hedges in the centre of the picture, I also found a suitable meadow yesterday with a north-westerly landing direction. So: the meadow is gone, but there are still several landing options left, so do check in good time to see if anything else has been ploughed up.

Surface: Field
Direction: 12/30
Viewing: 2023
Amended: 19 March 2023
Feedback:
- 14 March 2023, Jan Lyczywek: Site visit on 14 March 2023. An astonishingly good meadow! Flat and long. Both landing directions possible. When approaching from east to west, there is a single tree. The approach can be made either through narrow gaps to the left or right between this single tree and the adjacent rows of trees, or over the single tree – the field is long enough for this too. An approach from west to east is also possible and free of obstacles. Inspection video:

Location: Field
Direction: 12/31
Inspection: 2023
Updated: 2 April 2023
Reported hazards: Power lines/other cables
Feedback:
- 31 March 2023, Benedikt Bursian: Landing on 30 March 2023. Wheat field. As long as the crop is short or has been harvested, the field itself is definitely category A; the cable is not clearly visible during approach, but it is easy to fly over. Unfortunately, the farmer has had very bad experiences in the past with glider pilots who, after making off-field landings in this field, simply disappeared without taking responsibility for the damage to the crop. He therefore requests that the following notice be published here in three languages: PLEASE DO NOT LAND HERE – LANDOWNER STRONGLY DISAPPROVES OF OUTLANDINGS. PLEASE DO NOT LAND HERE – LANDOWNER STRONGLY DISAPPROVES OF OUTLANDINGS. DÉFENCE D'ATTERISSAGE DANS CE CHAMPS – PROPRIÉTAIRE N'ACCEPTE PLUS D'ATTERISSAGES

The area between the road and the strawberry field is suitable for landing, but narrow. There is also a portable toilet block and a stall at the southern end of the field. IT IS BETTER TO USE THE FIELD EAST OF THE ROAD – OR TO DIVERT TO EBNAT-KAPPEL.

The northern side of the meadow is significantly better, as it is both wider and smoother. Landing is no problem, even with large aircraft. An approach from the north is recommended, as there are no trees in the approach path and the meadow is better there. The wind appears to channel and was significantly stronger than in the surrounding area. (Mostly southerly wind)

Surface: Field
Direction: 18/36
Inspected: 2013
Amended: 10 April 2013
Feedback:
- 17 February 2004, Roland Henz: I landed here on 24 May 2003 as part of the AAC 2003. The area around Brunneck offers several landing options, including a model aeroplane airfield and what appears to be a disused airfield, although I have not yet been able to find any further information about it. The field where I landed is sufficiently long and presents no problems on approach; according to a former glider pilot from the area, it has been used on several occasions and is situated directly on a main road on the outskirts of Brunneck. Landing direction approx. 300°, towards the road/town entrance. As the maize was still young, this field might not be usable in summer. To the left and right of the field were meadows which, as far as I could see, had no ditches or fences. Perhaps someone from the region has more information on other landing options?

- 9 December 2023, Jan Lyczywek: Site visit on 13 November 1023. Just 2 km east of Ilanz. Possibly an alternative to Ilanz. Unfortunately, the large area – over 700 m in total length – is divided into individual plots at right angles to the direction of travel; depending on field use and the time of year, this could potentially be problematic. Partly arable farming. In the eastern third of the site, two strips of field run parallel to the valley, slightly elevated on a terrace; an underground power line crosses in a north-south direction. en\u001fOberfläche: Fields Richtung: 10/:
Surface: Fields
Direction: 10/-
Tour: 2024
Amended: 07.05.2024
Feedback:
- 5 May 2024, Manfred Schwegler: Visited in early April 2024. Still accessible. Unfortunately, there are still a few deep potholes, so it can be a bit bumpy when rolling out. The farmer has now put up a few posts to the north and south (only at the edge of the field) to mark the boundaries, but these do not get in the way given the width of the field. Unobstructed approach from the west across the track (best not to overrun it, as there is a slight dip). For an approach from the east (across the woodland), the field bordering it to the west is suitable. This one is slightly shorter – and also has a few holes in the ground. I’d avoid the field to the north! It contains stones, some as big as footballs.
- 13 June 2021, Manfred Schwegler: For the eastern (main) meadow, please REMOVE THE WEST ARROW from the OpenStreetMap map. As described in the text, only landing direction 10 (EAST) is possible on this meadow! When approaching from the east, the tall trees (wooded area) to the east of the road are an obstacle. However, a westward landing is possible on the adjacent meadows.
- 15 March 2021, Manfred Schwegler: Approach from the EAST! Due to a raised road and trees on the eastern edge of the field. Use the cross-field path (which can be driven over if necessary) as a threshold, and watch out for the tree to the right of the field. Otherwise, a clear approach. There are some slightly deeper mouse holes in places. In the event of a strong westerly wind, tall vegetation or if the field is flooded, choose a different field. In that case, however, BEWARE of the many telephone and power lines, particularly in the northern part of the area. In westerly winds, the fields bordering to the west would be suitable, but some of these also have slightly deeper holes. (As of May 2018)

Surface: Fields
Direction: 11/29
Tour: 2020
Amended: 28.02.2021
Reported hazards: Obstacles on the ground, power lines and other cables
Feedback:
- 21 February 2021, Andreas Hudler: North-east of Landeck, in the bend of the Inn. Small patches of land, alternating land use, 2 high-voltage power lines causing interference. The Venet cable car runs very close to the slope but does not cause any hindrance. Landing direction: west. Approach coming from Landeck along the southern slope, left-hand turn over the Venet cable car valley station, touchdown, then roll past the tree on the left. Landing direction east: Approach coming from Landeck along the northern slope, right-hand turn over the high-voltage pylon, short and steep descent, touchdown to the right of the tree. Quality: difficult, but OK
- 14 March 2009, Ulrich Plarre: Taking the high-voltage power lines into account, the meadows are suitable and can be classified as ‘yellow’ in terms of their characteristics. The main landing area shown on the map should be approached from west to east, with a crosswind approach over a smaller high-voltage power line.To the north, towards the Inn, there is a change in terrain and the high-voltage power line lies at a lower elevation. At the start of the landing field, a low vegetation line must be taken into account. For the marked alternatives, the cross-country routes are suitable for taxiing.

During a site visit on 16 July 2021, the north-western meadow north of the tree was suitable for landing, as was the south-eastern meadow (marked with a dotted line in the old SIP catalogue, and in yellow in the new one). The rest was planted with maize or marsh grass – and very damp there. A standard landing meadow in the Gail Valley, as one of the three areas is likely to have suitable vegetation most of the time.
- 23 March 2011, Thomas Keuschnig: initial feedback
- 17 March 2004, Thomas Keuschnig: Standard landing site in the Gail Valley, as one of the four areas always has suitable vegetation. The approach is straightforward.

Surface: Fields
Direction: 14/32
Tour: 2025
Amended: 20.04.2025
Reported hazards: Electricity and other cables
Feedback:
- 19 April 2025, Michael Buchthal: Site visit on 6 April 2025: The marked landing area must have been selected solely on the basis of a satellite image. During an on-site inspection, it would never occur to anyone that this depression, with cross-slopes exceeding 45° and no clear approach path, could be considered a landing site (Figures 1–4), let alone a Category A one!!! The field at the top of the hill is accessible from both directions, much like an aircraft carrier, but despite its length of approximately 250 metres, it is not a safe landing option due to undulating terrain and possible lee effects in stronger winds (Pictures 5–8). However, in an emergency, it offers an alternative to attempting a landing in the Kinzig Valley, which is very narrow in this area.

In the southern part, there are many transverse ditches running along the numerous field boundaries. The tarmac access road roughly in the middle of the area is, or was, a loading point for the air transport of high-voltage pylons. Mont Dauphin-St. Crépin airfield is only 5 km away; therefore, this is more of an emergency landing site. Feedback:
- 1 June 2024, Gerhard Wesp: Inspected on 19 May 2024 in the rain. Rising towards the west (direction of view in the photo) by approx. 10 m across the length of the meadow (approx. 4%). Land on bearing 24. Diagonally NE -> SW or at the right-hand edge of the meadow (otherwise a slight lateral slope). Slight undulations across the landing direction. During the site visit, there were no fences within the meadow, but there were some at the roadside. The presence of individual posts cannot be ruled out. There are alternatives in the immediate vicinity, but I could not find a better one. The terrain is generally very hilly.

Surface: Grass Land

Tilt: Slightly ascending
Tour: 2026
Amended: 25.06.2026
Reported hazards: uneven ground, electrical and other cables
Feedback:
- 25 June 2026, Benedikt Bursian: Landing on 25 June 2026. The field itself is in good condition; the power line is clearly visible on approach. The field slopes down towards the north, so a slightly angled approach is advisable.
- 27 June 2024, Ralf Paech: A good landing field, as described. Landing direction: west. The field slopes upwards towards the west. High-voltage power lines are clearly visible. Very friendly people in the area. There is a small restaurant nearby.
- 14 May 2023, Johannes Schwemmer: Visit by car on 12 May 2023 to the Unterwössen cross-country flying week. As already mentioned in the comment on the Embach meadow: it’s better to land at St. Johann im Pongau. If you can’t make it there or to Zell, this meadow is probably the lesser of two evils compared to the Embach meadow. See the video – landing under the power line seems to us to be possible only for experts in calm conditions. It’s better to come to a halt in the ditch and against the fence at just moderate speed; you can easily survive that, as a rollover is unlikely. And if it works out, you’ll stand there feeling like a hero. Good luck! As I said: a hero lands in St. Johann. Video:
- 11 April 2021, Martin Embacher: My initial impression has been confirmed: the meadow is relatively flat along an axis extending about 30 metres, but slopes at an average gradient of about 10 per cent from the north-east (towards the valley) to the south-west. There are two options: fly from the north-east over the power line (10 metres high) and land at the farm at the latest, or land beneath the power line. In the lower section, however, there are more uneven patches and the wooden pole does pose quite a tricky obstacle. Anyone needing to use the meadow will hopefully already be 100 per cent focused anyway – and that’s exactly what’s required.

Surface: Grass Land
